abstract: Self-evolving memory systems are unprecedentedly reshaping the evolutionary paradigm of large language model (LLM)-based agents. Prior work has predominantly relied on manually engineered memory architectures to store trajectories, distill experience, and synthesize reusable tools, enabling agents to evolve on the fly within environment interactions. However, this paradigm is fundamentally constrained by the staticity of the memory system itself: while memory facilitates agent-level evolving, the underlying memory architecture cannot be meta-adapted to diverse task contexts. To address this gap, we propose MemEvolve, a meta-evolutionary framework that jointly evolves agents' experiential knowledge and their memory architecture, allowing agent systems not only to accumulate experience but also to progressively refine how they learn from it. To ground MemEvolve in prior research and foster openness in future self-evolving systems, we introduce EvolveLab, a unified self-evolving memory codebase that distills twelve representative memory systems into a modular design space (encode, store, retrieve, manage), providing both a standardized implementation substrate and a fair experimental arena. Extensive evaluations on four challenging agentic benchmarks demonstrate that MemEvolve achieves (I) substantial performance gains, improving frameworks such as SmolAgent and Flash-Searcher by up to $17.06\%$; and (II) strong cross-task and cross-LLM generalization, designing memory architectures that transfer effectively across diverse benchmarks and backbone models.

keyInsights: Transitioning LLM agents from 'skillful' to 'adaptive' learners requires evolving not just experience, but the memory architecture itself.
Memory systems can be effectively decomposed into modular, interdependent components (Encode, Store, Retrieve, Manage) to enable systematic meta-evolution.
Multi-objective feedback (performance, cost, delay) can guide the principled evolution of memory architectures, leading to more efficient and effective designs.
originalProblem: Existing LLM agents employ static memory architectures, limiting their adaptability to diverse tasks and environments.
Current self-improving agents act as 'skillful learners' with a fixed learning strategy but cannot dynamically refine *how* they learn.
No single memory architecture is universally optimal, leading to suboptimal performance and restricted generalizability in open-ended settings.
results: MemEvolve consistently delivered performance improvements of 3.54% to 5.0% across all benchmarks, with peak gains of up to 17.06% for certain agent frameworks.

Demonstrated strong generalization capabilities, as memory systems evolved on one task/LLM/framework transferred effectively to unseen contexts, enhancing performance for new tasks, different LLMs (Kimi K2, DeepSeek V3.2), and other agent frameworks.
Achieved these performance gains with comparable API costs and execution delays to baseline systems, indicating efficient architectural evolution.
solution: Introduced a meta-evolutionary framework, MemEvolve, for joint evolution of agents' experiential knowledge and their underlying memory architecture.
Developed EvolveLab, a unified codebase defining a modular design space (Encode, Store, Retrieve, Manage) for memory systems, making architecture evolution tractable.
Implemented a 'Diagnose-and-Design' outer loop that selects, diagnoses defects, and generates new memory architectures based on empirical feedback (performance, cost, delay).
paper_summary.summary: OPPO AI Agent Team and LV-NUS lab developed MemEvolve, a meta-evolutionary framework that allows large language model agents to adaptively refine their memory architectures. This approach enables agents to consistently improve task performance by up to 17.06% and generalize across various tasks, LLMs, and agent frameworks.

title: MemEvolve: Meta-Evolution of Agent Memory Systems

abstract: Agentic Variation Operators (AVO) are a new family of evolutionary variation operators that replace the fixed mutation, crossover, and hand-designed heuristics of classical evolutionary search with autonomous coding agents. Rather than confining a language model to candidate generation within a prescribed pipeline, AVO instantiates variation as a self-directed agent loop that can consult the current lineage, a domain-specific knowledge base, and execution feedback to propose, repair, critique, and verify implementation edits. We evaluate AVO on attention, among the most aggressively optimized kernel targets in AI, on NVIDIA Blackwell (B200) GPUs. Over 7 days of continuous autonomous evolution on multi-head attention, AVO discovers kernels that outperform cuDNN by up to 3.5% and FlashAttention-4 by up to 10.5% across the evaluated configurations. The discovered optimizations transfer readily to grouped-query attention, requiring only 30 minutes of additional autonomous adaptation and yielding gains of up to 7.0% over cuDNN and 9.3% over FlashAttention-4. Together, these results show that agentic variation operators move beyond prior LLM-in-the-loop evolutionary pipelines by elevating the agent from candidate generator to variation operator, and can discover performance-critical micro-architectural optimizations that produce kernels surpassing state-of-the-art expert-engineered attention implementations on today's most advanced GPU hardware.

keyInsights: Elevating LLMs to autonomous "Agentic Variation Operators" enables them to engage in complex, iterative engineering workflows, moving beyond mere candidate generation to genuine code optimization.

Autonomous agents can perform deep hardware-level reasoning, including understanding synchronization, memory ordering, instruction pipeline scheduling, and register allocation, to achieve performance gains.
Continuous, self-directed evolution over extended durations can uncover highly optimized solutions that surpass expert-engineered baselines and are transferable to related computational tasks.
originalProblem: Existing LLM-augmented evolutionary search methods restrict LLMs to single-turn candidate generation, limiting their capacity for complex, iterative optimization workflows.
Optimizing performance-critical computational targets, such as GPU kernels for deep learning, demands intricate, multi-step engineering, which current LLM approaches do not fully support.

Achieving further performance gains on already aggressively optimized implementations like FlashAttention-4 and cuDNN requires deep, sustained, and iterative interaction with hardware specifics and development environments.
results: AVO-discovered multi-head attention (MHA) kernels on NVIDIA Blackwell B200 GPUs achieved up to 3.5% higher throughput than cuDNN and 10.5% higher than FlashAttention-4 for causal attention configurations.
The evolved MHA kernel was autonomously adapted to grouped-query attention (GQA) in approximately 30 minutes, showing up to 7.0% gains over cuDNN and 9.3% over FlashAttention-4 for causal GQA.

The agent identified specific micro-architectural optimizations, such as branchless accumulator rescaling, correction/MMA pipeline overlap, and register rebalancing across warp groups, illustrating its capacity for fine-grained hardware tuning.
solution: Introduce Agentic Variation Operators (AVO), which replace traditional evolutionary variation with an autonomous LLM-powered agent, granting it full agency over the optimization process.

The AVO agent is equipped with planning capabilities, persistent memory, and tool use (e.g., code editing, shell execution, documentation retrieval) to perform self-directed, multi-step optimization loops.

The agent continuously evolves solutions, performing internal edit-evaluate-diagnose cycles, and commits improved versions to a lineage, guided by a self-supervision mechanism to maintain progress over extended periods.
paper_summary.summary: Agentic Variation Operators (AVO) empower large language models to function as autonomous, iterative optimizers in evolutionary search for high-performance code. This framework successfully discovered multi-head attention kernels on NVIDIA Blackwell B200 GPUs that achieved up to 3.5% higher throughput than cuDNN and 10.5% higher than FlashAttention-4, alongside effective transferability to grouped-query attention.

title: AVO: Agentic Variation Operators for Autonomous Evolutionary Search

abstract: Large Language Model (LLM)-based agents have recently shown impressive capabilities in complex reasoning and tool use via multi-step interactions with their environments. While these agents have the potential to tackle complicated tasks, their problem-solving process, i.e., agents' interaction trajectory leading to task completion, remains underexploited. These trajectories contain rich feedback that can navigate agents toward the right directions for solving problems correctly. Although prevailing approaches, such as Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS), can effectively balance exploration and exploitation, they ignore the interdependence among various trajectories and lack the diversity of search spaces, which leads to redundant reasoning and suboptimal outcomes. To address these challenges, we propose SE-Agent, a Self-Evolution framework that enables Agents to optimize their reasoning processes iteratively. Our approach revisits and enhances former pilot trajectories through three key operations: revision, recombination, and refinement. This evolutionary mechanism enables two critical advantages: (1) it expands the search space beyond local optima by intelligently exploring diverse solution paths guided by previous trajectories, and (2) it leverages cross-trajectory inspiration to efficiently enhance performance while mitigating the impact of suboptimal reasoning paths. Through these mechanisms, SE-Agent achieves continuous self-evolution that incrementally improves reasoning quality. We evaluate SE-Agent on SWE-bench Verified to resolve real-world GitHub issues. Experimental results across five strong LLMs show that integrating SE-Agent delivers up to 55% relative improvement, achieving state-of-the-art performance among all open-source agents on SWE-bench Verified. Our code and demonstration materials are publicly available at this https URL.

organization_info.name: Chinese Academy of Sciences, Institute of Automation

keyInsights: Optimizing entire reasoning trajectories, rather than just individual steps or generating more samples, enables agents to learn and improve at a higher conceptual level.
A self-evolutionary paradigm, inspired by genetic algorithms, can effectively overcome LLM limitations like 'tunnel vision' and foster genuine diversity in problem-solving approaches.
Strategic cross-trajectory learning through explicit recombination operations is crucial for synthesizing novel solutions and leveraging collective insights across different reasoning attempts.
originalProblem: Existing LLM-based agents in multi-step reasoning often underexploit rich feedback from interaction trajectories.
Current methods typically treat individual reasoning trajectories independently, limiting search space diversity and leading to structurally similar, suboptimal solutions.
Agents frequently get stuck in local optima due to a lack of cross-trajectory learning and an inability to explore genuinely diverse solution paths.
results: SE-Agent consistently outperformed all baselines across five different LLMs on SWE-bench Verified, achieving state-of-the-art performance for open-source agents.
It delivered substantial relative Pass@1 improvements, including +112% for Llama-3.1-70b-Instruct (15.4% to 32.6%) and +80% for GPT-4o (22.4% to 40.4%).

An ablation study confirmed that all core modules (Revision, Recombination, Refinement) are essential for performance, with the Revision operation playing a particularly critical role in initial trajectory diversity.
solution: SE-Agent proposes a Self-Evolution framework that iteratively optimizes reasoning processes by treating trajectories as 'genotypes' that can be revised, recombined, and refined.
The Revision operation enhances individual trajectories through multi-planning exploration, mutation, and LLM-based reflection to identify and address weaknesses.
The Recombination operation facilitates collective evolution by combining strengths from different trajectories via crossover, transfer learning, and global restructuring to synthesize new paths.

The Refinement operation evaluates trajectories using a multi-dimensional reward function (task completion, reasoning quality, efficiency) and selects top-performing, diverse trajectories for the next iteration.
paper_summary.summary: A collaborative effort introduces SE-Agent, a self-evolutionary framework for LLM-based agents that optimizes multi-step reasoning trajectories via iterative revision, recombination, and refinement. This approach achieved state-of-the-art performance on SWE-bench Verified, improving Pass@1 by up to 112% for Llama-3.1-70b-Instruct and reaching 61.2% with Claude-3.7-Sonnet.

title: SE-Agent: Self-Evolution Trajectory Optimization in Multi-Step Reasoning with LLM-Based Agents

abstract: We introduce ShinkaEvolve: a new open-source framework leveraging large language models (LLMs) to advance scientific discovery with state-of-the-art performance and unprecedented efficiency. Recent advances in scaling inference time compute of LLMs have enabled significant progress in generalized scientific discovery. These approaches rely on evolutionary agentic harnesses that leverage LLMs as mutation operators to generate candidate solutions. However, current code evolution methods suffer from critical limitations: they are sample inefficient, requiring thousands of samples to identify effective solutions, and remain closed-source, hindering broad adoption and extension. ShinkaEvolve addresses these limitations, introducing three key innovations: a parent sampling technique balancing exploration and exploitation, code novelty rejection-sampling for efficient search space exploration, and a bandit-based LLM ensemble selection strategy. We evaluate ShinkaEvolve across diverse tasks, demonstrating consistent improvements in sample efficiency and solution quality. ShinkaEvolve discovers a new state-of-the-art circle packing solution using only 150 samples, designs high-performing agentic harnesses for AIME mathematical reasoning tasks, identifies improvements to ALE-Bench competitive programming solutions, and discovers novel mixture-of-expert load balancing loss functions that illuminate the space of optimization strategies. Our results demonstrate that ShinkaEvolve achieves broad applicability with exceptional sample efficiency. By providing open-source accessibility and cost-efficiency, this work democratizes open-ended discovery across diverse computational problems.

keyInsights: Systematically balancing exploration and exploitation through weighted sampling and adaptive LLM prioritization is crucial for achieving high sample efficiency in evolutionary search.
The iterative composition of "stepping stones"—suboptimal intermediate solutions—is a vital mechanism for reaching complex, high-quality solutions in program evolution.
Integrating robust novelty assessment, such as code embedding-based rejection, dramatically improves efficiency by filtering out redundant program explorations before evaluation.
originalProblem: Existing LLM-driven program evolution methods are sample-inefficient, demanding thousands of evaluations and incurring substantial computational costs and execution times.
Many leading LLM-based code evolution systems remain closed-source, which hinders reproducibility, limits community adoption, and restricts scientific collaboration and extensions.
results: Achieved a new state-of-the-art solution for the 26-circle packing problem using only 150 program evaluations, an improvement of orders of magnitude over prior methods.
Evolved agent scaffolds for AIME mathematical reasoning problems that significantly outperformed hand-designed baselines, demonstrating strong generalization to unseen tasks.
Improved high-performing C++ solutions for 10 competitive programming tasks (ALE-Bench) by an average of 2.3%, with one solution potentially ranking second on the AtCoder leaderboard.
Discovered a novel load balancing loss function for Mixture-of-Experts LLMs that yielded lower perplexity and improved downstream task performance on a 2.7B-parameter model.
solution: An adaptive parent and LLM sampling strategy balances exploration and exploitation, leveraging an archive of evaluated programs and an island model for diversity.
Code Novelty Rejection Sampling uses embeddings and an LLM-as-judge to prevent redundant evaluations of semantically similar program proposals, improving efficiency.
A bandit-based adaptive LLM ensemble dynamically prioritizes models based on their relative improvement contributions, complemented by a meta-scratchpad for online refinement and knowledge diffusion.
paper_summary.summary: ShinkaEvolve combines large language models with advanced evolutionary computation to create an open-source framework for efficient, open-ended program discovery. This approach achieves state-of-the-art performance across diverse tasks while dramatically reducing the number of computational evaluations required.

title: ShinkaEvolve: Towards Open-Ended And Sample-Efficient Program Evolution

abstract: The increasing adoption of Large Language Models (LLMs) has enabled AI scientists to perform complex end-to-end scientific discovery tasks requiring coordination of specialized roles, including idea generation and experimental execution. However, most state-of-the-art AI scientist systems rely on static, hand-designed pipelines and fail to adapt based on accumulated interaction histories. As a result, these systems overlook promising research directions, repeat failed experiments, and pursue infeasible ideas. To address this, we introduce EvoScientist, an evolving multi-agent AI scientist framework that continuously improves research strategies through persistent memory and self-evolution. EvoScientist comprises three specialized agents: a Researcher Agent (RA) for scientific idea generation, an Engineer Agent (EA) for experiment implementation and execution, and an Evolution Manager Agent (EMA) that distills insights from prior interactions into reusable knowledge. EvoScientist contains two persistent memory modules: (i) an ideation memory, which summarizes feasible research directions from top-ranked ideas while recording previously unsuccessful directions; and (ii) an experimentation memory, which captures effective data processing and model training strategies derived from code search trajectories and best-performing implementations. These modules enable the RA and EA to retrieve relevant prior strategies, improving idea quality and code execution success rates over time. Experiments show that EvoScientist outperforms 7 open-source and commercial state-of-the-art systems in scientific idea generation, achieving higher novelty, feasibility, relevance, and clarity via automatic and human evaluation. EvoScientist also substantially improves code execution success rates through multi-agent evolution, demonstrating persistent memory's effectiveness for end-to-end scientific discovery.

keyInsights: Scientific discovery can be framed as a continuous learning problem, where multi-agent systems adapt and improve strategies based on accumulated outcomes and failures.
The integration of propose-review-refine idea tree search and Elo-based tournament selection significantly enhances the novelty, feasibility, relevance, and clarity of generated scientific ideas.
Distilling insights from code generation trajectories and experimental results into experimentation memory measurably increases the mean execution success rate of experimental code.
originalProblem: Existing AI scientist systems operate with static execution pipelines, preventing continuous adaptation and improvement from accumulated experience.
Current AI agents for scientific discovery often pursue known failure patterns or overlook promising research directions due to fixed decision strategies and interaction patterns.
The absence of a mechanism for AI scientists to evolve their idea generation and experimental execution strategies over time by learning from prior successes and failures.
results: EvoScientist consistently outperformed 7 open-source and commercial baselines in idea generation, with average win rates ranging from +29.17% to +93.34% against open-sourced systems in novelty, feasibility, relevance, and clarity.
The system achieved a measurable improvement in code execution success rate, increasing the average from 34.39% to 44.56% across four experiment stages after Experiment Strategy Evolution.

Six complete research papers autonomously generated by EvoScientist were accepted to the International Conference on AI Scientists (ICAIS 2025), which had an overall acceptance rate of 31.71%, with one paper receiving a Best Paper Award.
solution: A self-evolving multi-agent system framework, EvoScientist, was designed with specialized Researcher, Engineer, and Evolution Manager Agents.
Two persistent memory modules, Ideation Memory and Experimentation Memory, store distilled insights from feasible research directions and effective execution strategies.

Multi-agent self-evolution mechanisms (Idea Direction Evolution, Idea Validation Evolution, Experiment Strategy Evolution) enable the system to learn from interaction histories and update memory modules for continuous improvement.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Huawei Technologies Co., Ltd. developed EvoScientist, a multi-agent framework that enables AI scientists to continuously improve their idea generation and experiment execution strategies by learning from past outcomes. The system produced higher-quality scientific ideas and demonstrated increased code execution success rates, leading to accepted research papers at an AI conference, including a Best Paper Award.

title: EvoScientist: Towards Multi-Agent Evolving AI Scientists for End-to-End Scientific Discovery

abstract: Open-ended self-improving agents can autonomously modify their own structural designs to advance their capabilities and overcome the limits of pre-defined architectures, thus reducing reliance on human intervention. We introduce Group-Evolving Agents (GEA), a new paradigm for open-ended self-improvements, which treats a group of agents as the fundamental evolutionary unit, enabling explicit experience sharing and reuse within the group throughout evolution. Unlike existing open-ended self-evolving paradigms that adopt tree-structured evolution, GEA overcomes the limitation of inefficient utilization of exploratory diversity caused by isolated evolutionary branches. We evaluate GEA on challenging coding benchmarks, where it significantly outperforms state-of-the-art self-evolving methods (71.0% vs. 56.7% on SWE-bench Verified, 88.3% vs. 68.3% on Polyglot) and matches or exceeds top human-designed agent frameworks (71.8% and 52.0% on two benchmarks, respectively). Analysis reveals that GEA more effectively converts early-stage exploratory diversity into sustained, long-term progress, achieving stronger performance under the same number of evolved agents. Furthermore, GEA exhibits consistent transferability across different coding models and greater robustness, fixing framework-level bugs in 1.4 iterations on average, versus 5 for self-evolving methods.

keyInsights: Treating a group of agents as the evolutionary unit, with explicit experience sharing, transforms transient exploratory diversity into sustained, cumulative progress for AI self-improvement.
AI agents, unlike biological organisms, are not bound by lineage and can directly share and aggregate 'trajectories, tools, and learned artifacts,' leading to more efficient evolution.

Meta-learning based self-improvement allows agents to autonomously evolve their own framework-level designs, matching or exceeding the performance of human-designed systems without direct human intervention.
originalProblem: Traditional AI systems rely on pre-defined architectures and continuous human intervention, limiting their ability for autonomous, open-ended self-improvement.

Existing open-ended self-evolving AI, often inspired by biological evolution, uses individual-centric, tree-structured approaches that isolate evolutionary branches, preventing effective experience sharing and long-term cumulative progress.
Exploratory diversity generated by individual agents in isolation often remains transient, failing to contribute to the collective and sustained advancement of AI capabilities.
results: GEA achieved a 71.0% success rate on SWE-bench Verified and 88.3% on Polyglot, significantly outperforming the state-of-the-art individual-centric Darwin Gödel Machine (DGM) baseline (56.7% and 68.3% respectively).
The autonomously evolved GEA agents demonstrated performance competitive with or superior to human-designed state-of-the-art frameworks, such as OpenHands + GPT-5 (71.8%) and Aider + GPT-5 (52.0%).

GEA exhibited enhanced robustness by repairing framework-level bugs in an average of 1.4 iterations, faster than DGM's 5 iterations, and demonstrated transferability of improvements across different backbone models (e.g., GPT and Claude series).
solution: GEA introduces a group-centric evolutionary paradigm where a collection of agents, rather than individuals, forms the fundamental unit of evolution.
The framework incorporates a Parent Group Selection mechanism that selects a diverse, high-performing group of agents using a balanced performance-novelty score.

Open-Ended Group Evolution explicitly enables intra-group experience sharing, aggregating all evolutionary traces from the parent group into a shared pool for every agent to utilize in its reflection and evolution modules.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at the University of California, Santa Barbara developed Group-Evolving Agents (GEA), a framework enabling open-ended self-improvement for AI systems by evolving groups of agents that share experiences. This group-centric approach led to a 71.0% success rate on SWE-bench Verified and 88.3% on Polyglot, surpassing individual-centric self-evolving methods and matching human-designed agent performance.

title: Group-Evolving Agents: Open-Ended Self-Improvement via Experience Sharing

abstract: Recent advancements in automatic code generation using large language model (LLM) agent have brought us closer to the future of automated software development. However, existing single-agent approaches face limitations in generating and improving large-scale, complex codebases due to constraints in context length. To tackle this challenge, we propose Self-Organized multi-Agent framework (SoA), a novel multi-agent framework that enables the scalable and efficient generation and optimization of large-scale code. In SoA, self-organized agents operate independently to generate and modify code components while seamlessly collaborating to construct the overall codebase. A key feature of our framework is the automatic multiplication of agents based on problem complexity, allowing for dynamic scalability. This enables the overall code volume to be increased indefinitely according to the number of agents, while the amount of code managed by each agent remains constant. We evaluate SoA on the HumanEval benchmark and demonstrate that, compared to a single-agent system, each agent in SoA handles significantly less code, yet the overall generated code is substantially greater. Moreover, SoA surpasses the powerful single-agent baseline by 5% in terms of Pass@1 accuracy.

keyInsights: Self-organization based on functional decomposition allows for a hierarchical multi-agent system where individual agents manage a constant, small amount of code, enabling indefinite scaling of total code volume.
Child agents observe their Mother agent's state and feedback, and Mother agents observe upper Mother feedback, facilitating collaborative and adaptive code improvement across the hierarchy.
The framework's ability to automatically multiply agents and delegate subfunctions enables parallel implementation and a distributed cognitive load, directly addressing context length limitations.
originalProblem: Existing single-agent LLM approaches for code generation are bottlenecked by the LLM's context length, limiting their ability to handle large and complex codebases.

Current multi-agent systems for software development typically focus on 'macro' occupational roles (e.g., project manager, QA) and do not address the 'micro' level scalability challenges of elemental code generation and modification.

The iterative process of code modification and self-debugging in large codebases suffers from context limitations, making it difficult for a single agent to consistently refine extensive code sections.
results: Self-Organized Agents (SoA) achieved a Pass@1 accuracy of 71.4% on the HumanEval benchmark, outperforming the state-of-the-art single-agent Reflexion (66.5%) by 5%.

Individual agents within SoA managed significantly less code (in characters and tokens) on average compared to a single Reflexion agent, while the total code generated by the entire SoA framework was substantially greater.

The findings validate SoA's core claim that it effectively scales the overall code volume by increasing the number of agents, maintaining a manageable and constant amount of code for each individual agent.
solution: The Self-Organized Agents (SoA) framework introduces two agent types: Child Agents, which implement specific functions, and Mother Agents, which generate and delegate subfunctions to new agents.

Agents self-organize in a hierarchical structure, with Mother agents decomposing the problem into smaller parts and delegating them, allowing for a distributed workload where no single agent needs to comprehend the entire codebase.

Code generation proceeds through decomposition, delegation, and asynchronous implementation, while code modification uses a top-down feedback propagation mechanism for iterative refinement across the hierarchy.
paper_summary.summary: TsukushiAI researchers developed Self-Organized Agents (SoA), a multi-agent framework designed to overcome context length limitations in LLM-based code generation by allowing agents to self-organize hierarchically and handle constant, small portions of code. SoA achieved a Pass@1 accuracy of 71.4% on HumanEval, outperforming single-agent Reflexion by 5% while demonstrating the ability to scale to significantly larger total code volumes.

title: Self-Organized Agents: A LLM Multi-Agent Framework toward Ultra Large-Scale Code Generation and Optimization

abstract: Self-evolution is a central research topic in enabling large language model (LLM)-based agents to continually improve their capabilities after pretraining. Recent research has witnessed a transition from reinforcement learning (RL)-free to RL-based methods. Current RL-based methods either rely on dense external reward signals or extract intrinsic reward signals from LLMs themselves. However, these approaches diverge from the self-evolution mechanisms observed in human intelligence, where individuals learn and improve through mutual discussion and collaboration. In this work, we introduce Co-Evolving Multi-Agent Systems (CoMAS), a novel framework that enables agents to improve autonomously by learning from inter-agent interactions without external supervision. CoMAS generates intrinsic rewards from rich discussion dynamics, employs an LLM-as-a-judge mechanism to formulate these rewards, and optimizes each agent's policy through RL, thereby enabling decentralized and scalable co-evolution. Experimental results demonstrate that CoMAS consistently outperforms untrained agents and achieves state-of-the-art performance across most evaluation settings. Ablation studies confirm the necessity of interaction-based reward signals and reveal promising scalability as the number and diversity of agents increase. These findings establish CoMAS as a novel and effective paradigm for self-evolution in LLM-based agents.

keyInsights: LLM agents can achieve self-evolution purely through intrinsic rewards derived from inter-agent interactions, mimicking human collective learning processes.
A carefully designed adversarial, complementary reward system is crucial for preventing reward hacking and training collapse, leading to stable and effective multi-agent learning.
The framework demonstrates positive scalability with an increased number of agents and benefits from agent diversity, allowing heterogeneous models to co-evolve and enhance overall performance.
originalProblem: Existing LLM-based agents are limited by static pre-trained capabilities, often struggling with genuine continual improvement.
Reinforcement Learning methods for agents typically rely on costly external rewards or individual-centric intrinsic rewards, failing to leverage collective intelligence.
Current paradigms do not mimic how human intelligence evolves through mutual discussion and collaboration without constant external evaluation.
results: CoMAS consistently improved performance across math, coding, science, and general knowledge benchmarks, outperforming untrained agents and baselines like MAPoRL and TTRL.
Achieved substantial gains in multi-agent collaborative setups, including +19.80% on GSM8K and +10.98% on HumanEval in the AutoGen setup, where other baselines faltered.
Ablation studies confirmed that the adversarial reward formulation is essential, as alternative schemes (e.g., 'w/o Evaluation', 'w/o Scoring') led to performance degradation or reward hacking.
solution: A multi-agent framework, CoMAS, is introduced, enabling hierarchical and decentralized interactions among heterogeneous LLM agents to generate solutions, evaluations, and scores.
An LLM-as-a-judge mechanism formulates intrinsic, complementary (zero-sum) rewards for solutions and evaluations, encouraging both correctness and critical feedback while preventing reward hacking.
Policy optimization for individual agents is performed using REINFORCE++ with token-level credit assignment and KL-divergence regularization to ensure stable and effective learning.
paper_summary.summary: The CoMAS framework enables large language model agents to self-evolve by learning solely from intrinsic inter-agent interaction rewards, eliminating the need for external supervision. This multi-agent system consistently improved performance across various benchmarks, achieving significant gains such as a 19.80% increase on GSM8K and 10.98% on HumanEval in multi-agent AutoGen settings.

title: CoMAS: Co-Evolving Multi-Agent Systems via Interaction Rewards

abstract: Multi-agent systems (MAS) leveraging the impressive capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs) hold significant potential for tackling complex tasks. However, most current MAS depend on manually designed agent roles and communication protocols. These manual designs often fail to align with the underlying LLMs' strengths and struggle to adapt to novel tasks. Recent automatic MAS approaches attempt to mitigate these limitations but typically necessitate a validation set for tuning and yield static MAS designs lacking adaptability during inference, while also removing the flexibility to reduce to simpler systems. We introduce MAS-ZERO, the first self-evolved, inference-time framework for automatic MAS design. MAS-ZERO employs meta-level design to iteratively design, critique, and refine MAS configurations tailored to each problem instance, without requiring a validation set. Critically, it enables dynamic problem decomposition and agent composition through meta-feedback on solvability and completeness, and reduction to simpler systems when appropriate. Experiments across reasoning (math and graduate-level QA), coding, and agentic (search-based) benchmarks, using both closed-source and open-source LLM backbones of varying sizes, demonstrate that MAS-ZERO outperforms strong manual and automatic MAS baselines. It achieves substantial average accuracy improvements of up to 16.69% on reasoning, 16.66% on coding, and 5.45% on agentic tasks, while maintaining cost efficiency.

keyInsights: Dynamic, inference-time MAS design with zero supervision, driven by iterative meta-design and meta-feedback, enables highly adaptive and effective multi-agent behaviors tailored to individual problem instances.

Explicit problem decomposition and the ability to dynamically simplify MAS complexity are crucial for handling varied problem difficulties and avoiding performance degradation from overly complex solutions.

A robust verification mechanism that can select from a diverse pool of solutions, including simpler baselines, is essential for achieving high performance and stability in stochastic LLM-based multi-agent systems.
originalProblem: Manual MAS design is rigid, often misaligned with underlying LLM capabilities, and struggles to scale to complex, diverse tasks.
Existing automatic MAS design methods typically rely on validation sets for tuning, which are frequently unavailable in real-world scenarios and result in static, non-adaptive architectures.

Current MAS approaches often lack the ability to dynamically decompose complex problems into sub-tasks or to simplify to a single-agent or less complex MAS when such strategies are more effective, leading to suboptimal performance or unnecessary overhead.
results: MAS-ZERO consistently outperformed strong manual and automatic MAS baselines across reasoning, coding, and agentic tasks, achieving up to 16.69% average accuracy improvements on reasoning benchmarks and 149% relative improvement on coding tasks.
The framework demonstrated superior cost-efficiency, often lying on the Pareto front when comparing accuracy-versus-cost trade-offs against other automatic MAS methods.

Ablation studies confirmed the critical contribution of each MAS-ZERO component: MAS-Init improved performance by 11.50%, MAS-Evolve by 7.60% (with problem decomposition contributing 8.96% and meta-feedback 8.38%), and MAS-Verify by a substantial 21.70%.
solution: MAS-ZERO employs a three-step, inference-time framework: MAS-Init executes predefined building blocks for initial solutions, MAS-Evolve iteratively refines MAS designs, and MAS-Verify selects the best candidate solution.

MAS-Evolve's meta-agent dynamically decomposes problems into sub-tasks and assigns sub-MAS, using meta-feedback (based on solvability and completeness) to iteratively adjust designs, informed by an experience library.

MAS-Verify robustly selects the optimal answer from a pool of candidates (from both MAS-Init and MAS-Evolve) by ranking solutions based on frequency and meta-agent review, ensuring dynamic reduction to simpler strategies when appropriate.
paper_summary.summary: MAS-ZERO presents a framework for designing Multi-Agent Systems (MAS) without supervision, operating entirely at inference time to dynamically adapt agent configurations per problem instance. It achieves superior performance and cost-efficiency across various tasks by iteratively refining MAS designs and intelligently selecting optimal solutions.

title: MAS-ZERO: Designing Multi-Agent Systems with Zero Supervision

abstract: The rapid progress of Large Language Models has advanced agentic systems in decision-making, coordination, and task execution. Yet, existing agentic system generation frameworks lack full autonomy, missing from-scratch agent generation, self-optimizing agent functionality, and collaboration, limiting adaptability and scalability. We propose SwarmAgentic, a framework for fully automated agentic system generation that constructs agentic systems from scratch and jointly optimizes agent functionality and collaboration as interdependent components through language-driven exploration. To enable efficient search over system-level structures, SwarmAgentic maintains a population of candidate systems and evolves them via feedback-guided updates, drawing inspiration from Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO). We evaluate our method on six real-world, open-ended, and exploratory tasks involving high-level planning, system-level coordination, and creative reasoning. Given only a task description and an objective function, SwarmAgentic outperforms all baselines, achieving a +261.8% relative improvement over ADAS on the TravelPlanner benchmark, highlighting the effectiveness of full automation in structurally unconstrained tasks. This framework marks a significant step toward scalable and autonomous agentic system design, bridging swarm intelligence with fully automated system multi-agent generation. Our code is publicly released at this https URL.

keyInsights: Traditional swarm intelligence algorithms like PSO can be successfully adapted from continuous numerical spaces to discrete, symbolic, language-based design spaces for complex AI system generation.
Achieving full autonomy in agentic system generation requires jointly optimizing individual agent functionalities and their inter-agent collaboration strategies within a unified process.
Incorporating explicit, LLM-driven "flaw identification" and "failure-driven adjustments" significantly enhances the efficiency and effectiveness of optimization in symbolic design spaces.
originalProblem: Existing LLM-based agentic system frameworks lack full autonomy, often relying on predefined roles, templates, or seed agents, limiting adaptability to novel tasks.
Current methods typically offer limited self-optimization for agent functionality or static inter-agent collaboration patterns, hindering their ability to evolve.
Manually designing effective agents and their complex collaboration strategies for open-ended, structurally unconstrained tasks is prohibitively difficult and not scalable.
results: SwarmAgentic consistently outperformed standard prompting methods and leading automated agentic system generation frameworks on six diverse tasks, achieving a +261.8% relative improvement over ADAS on the TravelPlanner benchmark.
The framework demonstrates robust cross-model transferability, where agentic systems discovered using one LLM generalize and perform well when executed by other LLMs.
Ablation studies confirmed the critical importance of Failure-Driven Adjustments, Agent-Level Adaptation, and Collaborative Structures Reconfiguration for the system's performance.
solution: SwarmAgentic reinterprets Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) for a discrete, language-based, symbolic design space, where each "particle" represents a complete agentic system.
It automates agent generation, functionality optimization, and collaboration structure reconfiguration through LLM-driven textual transformation plans.
A failure-aware velocity update mechanism identifies specific flaws (agent or collaborative structure flaws) and uses this feedback to guide subsequent optimization steps.
paper_summary.summary: SwarmAgentic introduces a framework that automates the generation of multi-agent systems from a task description, leveraging a novel reinterpretation of Particle Swarm Optimization for symbolic search spaces. This approach synthesizes and jointly optimizes agent functionalities and collaboration strategies, leading to improved performance on complex, open-ended tasks compared to existing methods.

title: SwarmAgentic: Towards Fully Automated Agentic System Generation via Swarm Intelligence

abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have enabled remarkable advances in automated task-solving with multi-agent systems. However, most existing LLM-based multi-agent approaches rely on predefined agents to handle simple tasks, limiting the adaptability of multi-agent collaboration to different scenarios. Therefore, we introduce AutoAgents, an innovative framework that adaptively generates and coordinates multiple specialized agents to build an AI team according to different tasks. Specifically, AutoAgents couples the relationship between tasks and roles by dynamically generating multiple required agents based on task content and planning solutions for the current task based on the generated expert agents. Multiple specialized agents collaborate with each other to efficiently accomplish tasks. Concurrently, an observer role is incorporated into the framework to reflect on the designated plans and agents' responses and improve upon them. Our experiments on various benchmarks demonstrate that AutoAgents generates more coherent and accurate solutions than the existing multi-agent methods. This underscores the significance of assigning different roles to different tasks and of team cooperation, offering new perspectives for tackling complex tasks. The repository of this project is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Dynamically generating and coordinating specialized AI agents through a multi-stage refinement process enhances LLM performance in complex tasks.
A drafting stage with observer agents helps ensure the rationality of generated agents and the coherence of execution plans.
Integrating self-refinement, collaborative refinement, and dynamic memory mechanisms allows agents to improve individually and collectively, mimicking human team collaboration.
originalProblem: Existing LLM-based multi-agent systems rely on predefined or handcrafted agents, limiting their adaptability to novel and complex tasks.
Manually configuring agent roles and communication protocols for diverse problems is resource-intensive and impractical.

Standalone LLMs often struggle with complex, multi-faceted tasks due to issues like hallucination, lack of elaborate reasoning, and difficulty combining diverse domain knowledge for long-horizon planning.
results: AutoAgents achieved a 76.3% win rate against GPT-4 on FairEval for open-ended question answering and 62.5% on HumanEval, outperforming individual LLMs.
On trivia creative writing tasks, AutoAgents improved performance by 9.9% for N=5 and 10.8% for N=10 over standard prompting, demonstrating superior knowledge acquisition and integration.

Ablation studies confirmed the critical roles of observer agents (3% performance drop), self-refinement (3% drop), collaborative refinement (2% drop), and dynamic memory (1% drop) in overall performance.
solution: AutoAgents introduces a two-stage framework: a Drafting Stage for automatic agent and plan generation, and an Execution Stage for task completion and refinement.
The Drafting Stage employs a Planner agent and two observer agents (Agent Observer and Plan Observer) to collaboratively synthesize an optimal agent team and execution plan.

The Execution Stage utilizes an Action Observer to manage task allocation and incorporates self-refinement for individual agents and collaborative refinement for the team, supported by a multi-tiered memory system.
paper_summary.summary: AutoAgents is a framework that automatically generates and coordinates specialized AI agents tailored to specific tasks, improving upon limitations of static multi-agent systems. It demonstrates enhanced performance over individual LLMs and other multi-agent approaches in benchmarks such as open-ended question answering and creative writing, achieving up to a 76.3% win rate against GPT-4.

title: AutoAgents: A Framework for Automatic Agent Generation

abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are catalyzing the development of autonomous AI research agents for scientific and engineering discovery. We present FM Agent, a novel and general-purpose multi-agent framework that leverages a synergistic combination of LLM-based reasoning and large-scale evolutionary search to address complex real-world challenges. The core of FM Agent integrates several key innovations: 1) a cold-start initialization phase incorporating expert guidance, 2) a novel evolutionary sampling strategy for iterative optimization, 3) domain-specific evaluators that combine correctness, effectiveness, and LLM-supervised feedback, and 4) a distributed, asynchronous execution infrastructure built on Ray. Demonstrating broad applicability, our system has been evaluated across diverse domains, including operations research, machine learning, GPU kernel optimization, and classical mathematical problems. FM Agent reaches state-of-the-art results autonomously, without human interpretation or tuning -- 1976.3 on ALE-Bench (+5.2\%), 43.56\% on MLE-Bench (+4.0pp), up to 20x speedups on KernelBench, and establishes new state-of-the-art(SOTA) results on several classical mathematical problems. Beyond academic benchmarks, FM Agent shows considerable promise for both large-scale enterprise R\&D workflows and fundamental scientific research, where it can accelerate innovation, automate complex discovery processes, and deliver substantial engineering and scientific advances with broader societal impact.
title: The FM Agent

abstract:
The past two years have witnessed the evolution of large language model
(LLM)-based multi-agent systems from labor-intensive manual design to partial
automation (\textit{e.g.}, prompt engineering, communication topology) and
eventually to fully automated design. However, existing agentic automation
pipelines often lack LLM heterogeneity and focus on single-objective
performance optimization, limiting their potential to combine weaker models for
more customized and cost-effective solutions. To address this challenge, we
propose EvoFlow, a niching evolutionary algorithm-based framework to
automatically search a population of heterogeneous and complexity-adaptive
agentic workflows, rather than a single homogeneous, complex workflow.
Technically, EvoFlow performs \textit{(1) tag-based retrieval} to extract
parent workflows from an agentic population, evolves new workflows through
\textit{(2) crossover} and \textit{(3) mutation}, and employs \textit{(4)
niching-based selection} to maintain population diversity and quality.
Extensive evaluations across seven benchmarks demonstrate that EvoFlow is:
\textbf{(I) diverse}, evolving a population of workflows ranging from simple
I/O tasks to complex multi-turn interactions; \textbf{(II) high-performing},
outperforming previous handcrafted and automated workflows by
$1.23\%\sim29.86\%$; \textbf{(III) economical}, surpassing powerful
\llmname{o1-preview} at $12.4\%$ of its inference cost using weaker open-source
models.

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong

keyInsights: Multi-objective optimization balancing cost and performance enables more efficient resource utilization
Maintaining workflow diversity through niching helps address varying task complexities
Heterogeneous LLM combinations can achieve better results than single high-end models
originalProblem: Existing multi-agent systems rely on manual workflow design and homogeneous LLMs, leading to high costs and limited adaptability
Current approaches lack mechanisms to balance performance and cost while maintaining workflow diversity across varying task complexities
results: Outperforms previous approaches by 1.23%~29.86% across seven benchmarks
Achieves better performance than GPT-4 while reducing costs by 87.6%
Successfully evolves workflows ranging from simple I/O to complex multi-turn interactions
Demonstrates effective cross-domain optimization and complexity adaptation
solution: Automated evolution of workflows using tag-based retrieval, crossover/mutation operations, and niching-based selection
Hierarchical search space combining invoking nodes, operator nodes, and complete workflows with heterogeneous LLM integration
paper_summary.summary: EvoFlow introduces a groundbreaking evolutionary framework that automatically designs diverse and adaptive multi-agent workflows, achieving superior performance to GPT-4 at just 12.4% of the cost through innovative combination of heterogeneous language models and multi-objective optimization techniques developed by researchers from leading Chinese institutions.

title: EvoFlow: Evolving Diverse Agentic Workflows On The Fly

abstract:
Recent advancements in Large Language Models (LLMs) have led to a rapid
growth of agentic systems capable of handling a wide range of complex tasks.
However, current research largely relies on manual, task-specific design,
limiting their adaptability to novel tasks. In this paper, we introduce a new
research problem: Modularized LLM Agent Search (MoLAS). We propose a modular
design space that abstracts existing LLM agent designs into four fundamental
modules with uniform IO interface: Planning, Reasoning, Tool Use, and Memory.
Building on this design space, we present a novel LLM agent search framework
called AgentSquare, which introduces two core mechanisms, i.e., module
evolution and recombination, to efficiently search for optimized LLM agents. To
further accelerate the process, we design a performance predictor that uses
in-context surrogate models to skip unpromising agent designs. Extensive
experiments across six benchmarks, covering the diverse scenarios of web,
embodied, tool use and game applications, show that AgentSquare substantially
outperforms hand-crafted agents, achieving an average performance gain of 17.2%
against best-known human designs. Moreover, AgentSquare can generate
interpretable design insights, enabling a deeper understanding of agentic
architecture and its impact on task performance. We believe that the modular
design space and AgentSquare search framework offer a platform for fully
exploiting the potential of prior successful designs and consolidating the
collective efforts of research community. Code repo is available at
this https URL

keyInsights: A modular design space with standardized interfaces enables automated search and facilitates the reuse and combination of LLM agent components.
Automated search, driven by iterative module evolution and recombination, can efficiently explore complex agent architectures.
An in-context performance predictor significantly reduces the cost of evaluating LLM agents, making large-scale automated design practical.
originalProblem: Current LLM agent development relies on manual, time-consuming, and labor-intensive design, leading to sub-optimal and specialized solutions.
Lack of a unified framework hinders the reusability and integration of successful components from different LLM agents.
The vast design space for LLM agent architectures makes efficient exploration challenging, while evaluation costs are high.
results: Achieved an average of 17.2% performance gain over the best human-designed agents across six diverse benchmarks, including Webshop, ALFWorld, and SciWorld.
Demonstrated a more efficient search trajectory than existing module-level, prompt-level, and general agent search methods (ADAS), reaching higher performance with the same LLM token budget.
Validated the effectiveness of its core mechanisms, showing module evolution, recombination, and the performance predictor are crucial for reducing evaluation costs and improving search efficiency.
solution: Defines a Modularized LLM Agent Search (MoLAS) space, categorizing agents into four core modules (Planning, Reasoning, Tool Use, Memory) with standardized I/O interfaces.

Employs an iterative search algorithm that alternates between Module Evolution (generating novel module designs using an LLM programmer) and Module Recombination (combining existing modules with an LLM proposer).
Incorporates an in-context LLM performance predictor to estimate agent performance, significantly reducing costly real-world evaluations.
paper_summary.summary: AgentSquare, a framework from Tsinghua University, automates the design and optimization of Large Language Model agents using a Modularized LLM Agent Search approach. The system discovers agent configurations that achieve an average of 17.2% higher performance compared to human-designed agents across six diverse benchmarks, while also using a performance predictor to substantially reduce evaluation costs.

title: AgentSquare: Automatic LLM Agent Search in Modular Design Space

abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have emerged as powerful operators for evolutionary search, yet the design of efficient search scaffolds remains ad hoc. While promising, current LLM-in-the-loop systems lack a systematic approach to managing the evolutionary process. We identify three distinct failure modes: Context Pollution, where experiment history biases future candidate generation; Mode Collapse, where agents stagnate in local minima due to poor exploration-exploitation balance; and Weak Collaboration, where rigid crossover strategies fail to leverage parallel search trajectories effectively. We introduce Progress-Aware Consistent Evolution (PACEvolve), a framework designed to robustly govern the agent's context and search dynamics, to address these challenges. PACEvolve combines hierarchical context management (HCM) with pruning to address context pollution; momentum-based backtracking (MBB) to escape local minima; and a self-adaptive sampling policy that unifies backtracking and crossover for dynamic search coordination (CE), allowing agents to balance internal refinement with cross-trajectory collaboration. We demonstrate that PACEvolve provides a systematic path to consistent, long-horizon self-improvement, achieving state-of-the-art results on LLM-SR and KernelBench, while discovering solutions surpassing the record on Modded NanoGPT.

keyInsights: Systematic context management, including hierarchical decomposition of ideas and active pruning, is crucial for maintaining a high signal-to-noise ratio and preventing context pollution over long evolutionary horizons.
Adaptive monitoring of search progress, using metrics like relative improvement momentum, can effectively identify and mitigate mode collapse by triggering targeted interventions like backtracking.

Dynamically balancing internal refinement with external knowledge transfer in parallel searches, guided by the relative and absolute progress of each island, leads to more efficient and synergistic multi-agent evolution.
originalProblem: LLM-driven evolutionary search agents often exhibit suboptimal and unstable performance, characterized by high variance and lack of systematic management.
Context pollution occurs when search history saturates with failed hypotheses, degrading the quality of future idea generation and decision-making.
Agents frequently suffer from mode collapse, struggling to balance exploration and exploitation, which leads to stagnation in local optima rather than discovering diverse solutions.
results: PACEvolve-Single achieved state-of-the-art Log10 NMSE on the Symbolic Regression benchmark, while PACEvolve-Multi discovered three solutions with Log10 NMSE lower than -8.

On KernelBench, PACEvolve-Single outperformed existing evolutionary frameworks across all 16 GPU kernels, and PACEvolve-Multi further improved performance in 81.25% of cases, achieving speedups up to 17.38x over PyTorch.

The framework demonstrated robust automated engineering capabilities in the highly optimized Modded NanoGPT environment, discovering a series of improvements that reduced training time from 142.8s to 140.2s.
solution: Hierarchical Context Management (HCM) reforms how LLM agents store and utilize evolutionary history through bi-level pruning and persistent memory for failures.
Momentum-based Backtracking (MBB) introduces an adaptive mechanism using relative progress momentum to detect stagnation and trigger hard escapes from local minima.

Self-Adaptive Collaborative Evolution Sampling (CE) unifies backtracking and crossover into a dynamic action selection policy for multi-island setups, ensuring adaptive knowledge transfer based on progress.
paper_summary.summary: PACEvolve presents a principled framework for Large Language Model-driven evolutionary search, systematically addressing challenges like context pollution, mode collapse, and weak collaboration. This framework achieves state-of-the-art performance in symbolic regression, significantly optimizes GPU kernel code, and demonstrates automated research capabilities in complex engineering environments like Modded NanoGPT.

title: PACEvolve: Enabling Long-Horizon Progress-Aware Consistent Evolution

abstract: The transition from static Large Language Models (LLMs) to self-improving agents is hindered by the lack of structured reasoning in traditional evolutionary approaches. Existing methods often struggle with premature convergence and inefficient exploration in high-dimensional code spaces. To address these challenges, we introduce LoongFlow, a self-evolving agent framework that achieves state-of-the-art solution quality with significantly reduced computational costs. Unlike "blind" mutation operators, LoongFlow integrates LLMs into a cognitive "Plan-Execute-Summarize" (PES) paradigm, effectively mapping the evolutionary search to a reasoning-heavy process. To sustain long-term architectural coherence, we incorporate a hybrid evolutionary memory system. By synergizing Multi-Island models with MAP-Elites and adaptive Boltzmann selection, this system theoretically balances the exploration-exploitation trade-off, maintaining diverse behavioral niches to prevent optimization stagnation. We instantiate LoongFlow with a General Agent for algorithmic discovery and an ML Agent for pipeline optimization. Extensive evaluations on the AlphaEvolve benchmark and Kaggle competitions demonstrate that LoongFlow outperforms leading baselines (e.g., OpenEvolve, ShinkaEvolve) by up to 60% in evolutionary efficiency while discovering superior solutions. LoongFlow marks a substantial step forward in autonomous scientific discovery, enabling the generation of expert-level solutions with reduced computational overhead.

keyInsights: Integrating LLMs into a cognitive PES paradigm transforms random, blind mutation into a directed, hypothesis-testing evolutionary process, significantly improving search efficiency and solution quality.

A structured feedback loop, enabled by the Summarizer's abductive reflection and stored insights, allows the system to learn from past errors and accumulate "evolutionary wisdom," preventing cyclical mistakes.

A multi-layered hybrid memory system (multi-island, MAP-Elites, adaptive selection) is crucial for maintaining population diversity and preventing premature convergence, leading to a robust and stable search in complex spaces.
originalProblem: Current LLM-based evolutionary optimization methods suffer from inefficient exploration, often relying on high-volume random mutations that lead to computationally expensive "random walk" behavior.
Existing frameworks face diversity collapse and premature convergence to local optima due to a lack of explicit diversity management mechanisms, hindering long-term progress.
These systems typically lack reflexive memory and structured feedback, resulting in "memory-less" searchers that repeatedly make similar errors across generations.
results: Achieved state-of-the-art solution quality on AlphaEvolve Suite problems, e.g., discovering a solution for Autocorrelation II with a score of 0.9027, surpassing AlphaEvolve's 0.8962.

Demonstrated over 60% improvement in evolutionary efficiency, needing an average of 258 evaluations to reach a target score ">=" 0.99 for Circle Packing, compared to OpenEvolve's 783 evaluations, with a 100% success rate.
Secured 14 Gold Medals across various MLEBench competitions and consistently broke theoretical performance barriers (score ">" 1.0) within strict computational budgets where baselines failed.
solution: Introduces the "Plan-Execute-Summarize" (PES) cognitive paradigm, where a Planner strategizes, an Executor implements and locally verifies, and a Summarizer reflects to generate structured insights for a long-term cognitive memory.

Develops a Hybrid Evolutionary Memory System incorporating a multi-island distributed topology, MAP-Elites with feature grids for niche preservation, and adaptive Boltzmann selection for dynamic exploration-exploitation balance.
Frames the evolutionary process as a Markov Decision Process, with the LLM acting as a learnable semantic operator that guides the search and learning based on structured feedback.
paper_summary.summary: Baidu Inc. developed LoongFlow, a self-evolving agent framework that integrates Large Language Models (LLMs) into a "Plan-Execute-Summarize" (PES) cognitive loop and a hybrid evolutionary memory system for directed evolutionary search. This framework achieved state-of-the-art solution quality on algorithmic discovery benchmarks and demonstrated over 60% improvement in evolutionary efficiency across various tasks, notably requiring fewer evaluations to reach target scores.

title: LoongFlow: Directed Evolutionary Search via a Cognitive Plan-Execute-Summarize Paradigm

abstract:
The rise of powerful large language models (LLMs) has spurred a new trend in
building LLM-based autonomous agents for solving complex tasks, especially
multi-agent systems. Despite the remarkable progress, we notice that existing
works are heavily dependent on human-designed frameworks, which greatly limits
the functional scope and scalability of agent systems. How to automatically
extend the specialized agent to multi-agent systems to improve task-solving
capability still remains a significant challenge. In this paper, we introduce
EvoAgent, a generic method to automatically extend specialized agents to
multi-agent systems via the evolutionary algorithm, thereby improving the
effectiveness of LLM-based agents in solving tasks. Specifically, we consider
the existing agent frameworks as the initial individual and then apply a series
of evolutionary operators (e.g., mutation, crossover, selection, etc.) to
generate multiple agents with diverse settings. Experimental results across
various tasks show that EvoAgent can significantly enhance the task-solving
capability of LLM-based agents, and can be generalized to any LLM-based agent
framework to extend them into multi-agent systems. Resources are available at
this https URL

keyInsights: Evolutionary algorithms, when facilitated by LLMs, provide an effective paradigm for automatically generating and optimizing diverse multi-agent systems from existing specialized agents.
Automating the agent generation process addresses the limitations of human-designed frameworks, enhancing scalability, functional scope, and adaptability of LLM-based agents.

Automatically generated specialized agents, through their collaborative intelligence, lead to superior task-solving capabilities across various domains compared to single agents or less specialized multi-agent setups.
originalProblem: Existing multi-agent systems for large language models (LLMs) are heavily dependent on human-designed frameworks, including predefined roles, skills, and prompt settings.
This reliance on manual design severely limits the functional scope, scalability, and adaptability of current LLM-based agent systems for complex, open-world tasks.
A generic and automatic method for extending specialized LLM agents into diverse and effective multi-agent collaborations has been lacking.
results: EVOAGENT consistently and significantly improved LLM performance across various NLP benchmarks (e.g., achieving 77.00% on Logic Grid Puzzle with GPT-4) and multi-modal understanding tasks.

It successfully extended interactive agents, improving performance in dynamic scientific problem-solving simulations (ScienceWorld) and demonstrated superior results in real-world complex planning tasks like TravelPlanner.
Ablation studies confirmed the critical role of specialized agent generation and the quality-check module for maintaining agent diversity and achieving optimal task performance.
solution: Formulates multi-agent generation as an evolutionary process, treating existing specialized LLM agents as "individuals" whose settings can "evolve" across generations.
Leverages large language models (LLMs) to automatically implement evolutionary operators such as Crossover (refining skills) and Mutation (introducing diversity).
Employs an LLM-based quality-check module for Selection, ensuring that only high-quality, distinct agents are chosen for the next generation, refining the agent population iteratively.
paper_summary.summary: EVOAGENT introduces an evolutionary algorithm-inspired method for automatically generating specialized multi-agent systems from existing LLM-based agents, enhancing their task-solving capabilities. This approach consistently improved performance across diverse benchmarks, including achieving 77.00% on Logic Grid Puzzle and higher constraint adherence in complex real-world planning tasks like TravelPlanner, compared to human-designed and other automated baselines.

title: EvoAgent: Towards Automatic Multi-Agent Generation via Evolutionary  Algorithms

abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have enabled automated heuristic design (AHD) for combinatorial optimization problems (COPs), but existing frameworks' reliance on fixed evolutionary rules and static prompt templates often leads to myopic heuristic generation, redundant evaluations, and limited reasoning about how new heuristics should be derived. We propose a novel multi-agent reasoning framework, referred to as Planning through World Model for Automated Heuristic Design via Self-Evolving LLMs (PathWise), which formulates heuristic generation as a sequential decision process over an entailment graph serving as a compact, stateful memory of the search trajectory. This approach allows the system to carry forward past decisions and reuse or avoid derivation information across generations. A policy agent plans evolutionary actions, a world model agent generates heuristic rollouts conditioned on those actions, and critic agents provide routed reflections summarizing lessons from prior steps, shifting LLM-based AHD from trial-and-error evolution toward state-aware planning through reasoning. Experiments across diverse COPs show that PathWise converges faster to better heuristics, generalizes across different LLM backbones, and scales to larger problem sizes.

keyInsights: Modeling heuristic generation as a stateful sequential decision process over an entailment graph allows LLMs to learn, adapt, and reason more deeply about evolutionary strategies.

Coordinated multi-agent LLM systems, with specialized roles for planning, execution, and reflective criticism, can significantly enhance performance and self-evolution in complex algorithmic design tasks.
Strategic diversity mechanisms are essential for mitigating LLM biases and ensuring broad exploration of the heuristic search space, leading to more robust and higher-quality solutions.
originalProblem: Manually designing effective heuristics for NP-hard Combinatorial Optimization Problems (COPs) is costly, time-consuming, and requires significant domain expertise, with limited generalization across problem variants.

Existing LLM-based Automated Heuristic Design (AHD) methods suffer from myopic heuristic generation, relying on fixed evolutionary rules and static prompts, which limits learning and adaptive strategy development.
Current LLM-AHD approaches lack a stateful memory of heuristic derivations and outcomes, leading to inefficiencies like redundant evaluations and an inability to build upon past insights effectively.
results: PathWise consistently outperforms state-of-the-art LLM-based AHD baselines and manual heuristics across diverse COPs (e.g., TSP, KP, CVRP, MKP) and search frameworks, achieving Mean Relative Gap Improvements (MRGI) up to 89.35% in some cases.

The framework demonstrates faster convergence, lower performance variance, and strong generalizability to out-of-domain and larger problem instances, even outperforming neural solvers for some problem sizes.

Ablation studies confirm the critical role of both Policy and World Model Critic agents for strategic feedback and prompt-level diversity mechanisms for effective exploration, with PathWise maintaining competitive computational costs despite superior performance.
solution: The approach formulates heuristic discovery as a sequential decision process over an entailment graph, serving as a compact, stateful memory that records derivation rationale, parent information, and performance history.

A multi-agent LLM framework is employed, featuring a Policy agent for high-level planning, a World Model agent for low-level execution and heuristic generation, and two Critic agents that provide reflective feedback on evolutionary strategy and code synthesis quality.

A hybrid graph-based and population-based approach is used, incorporating diversity mechanisms such as time-varying prompt perturbation and state shuffling to encourage exploration and prevent premature convergence.
paper_summary.summary: PathWise introduces a multi-agent LLM framework that frames heuristic discovery as a sequential decision process over an entailment graph, enabling state-aware planning for automated heuristic design. This approach leads to the discovery of stronger, more generalizable heuristics with faster convergence across diverse combinatorial optimization problems compared to current LLM-based AHD methods.

title: PathWise: Planning through World Model for Automated Heuristic Design via Self-Evolving LLMs

abstract: We introduce CodeEvolve, an open-source framework that combines large language models (LLMs) with evolutionary search to synthesize high-performing algorithmic solutions. CodeEvolve couples an islands-based genetic algorithm with modular LLM orchestration, using execution feedback and task-specific metrics to guide selection and variation. Exploration and exploitation are balanced through context-aware recombination, adaptive meta-prompting, and targeted refinement of promising solutions. We evaluate CodeEvolve on benchmarks used to assess Google DeepMind's AlphaEvolve, and include direct comparisons with popular open-source frameworks for algorithmic discovery and heuristic design. Our results show that CodeEvolve achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance on several tasks, with open-weight models often matching or exceeding closed-source baselines at a fraction of the compute cost. We provide extensive ablations, practical hyperparameter guidance, and release our framework and experimental results at this https URL.
title: CodeEvolve: an open source evolutionary coding agent for algorithmic discovery and optimization

abstract:
We introduce a novel large language model (LLM)-driven agent framework, which
iteratively refines queries and filters contextual evidence by leveraging
dynamically evolving knowledge. A defining feature of the system is its
decoupling of external sources from an internal knowledge cache that is
progressively updated to guide both query generation and evidence selection.
This design mitigates bias-reinforcement loops and enables dynamic, trackable
search exploration paths, thereby optimizing the trade-off between exploring
diverse information and maintaining accuracy through autonomous agent
decision-making. Our approach is evaluated on a broad range of open-domain
question answering benchmarks, including multi-step tasks that mirror
real-world scenarios where integrating information from multiple sources is
critical, especially given the vulnerabilities of LLMs that lack explicit
reasoning or planning capabilities. The results show that the proposed system
not only outperforms single-step baselines regardless of task difficulty but
also, compared to conventional iterative retrieval methods, demonstrates
pronounced advantages in complex tasks through precise evidence-based reasoning
and enhanced efficiency. The proposed system supports both competitive and
collaborative sharing of updated context, enabling multi-agent extension. The
benefits of multi-agent configurations become especially prominent as task
difficulty increases. The number of convergence steps scales with task
difficulty, suggesting cost-effective scalability.
title: Knowledge-Aware Iterative Retrieval for Multi-Agent Systems

abstract: Multi-agent systems (MAS) have emerged as a powerful paradigm for orchestrating large language models (LLMs) and specialized tools to collaboratively address complex tasks. However, existing MAS frameworks often require manual workflow configuration and lack native support for dynamic evolution and performance optimization. In addition, many MAS optimization algorithms are not integrated into a unified framework. In this paper, we present EvoAgentX, an open-source platform that automates the generation, execution, and evolutionary optimization of multi-agent workflows. EvoAgentX employs a modular architecture consisting of five core layers: the basic components, agent, workflow, evolving, and evaluation layers. Specifically, within the evolving layer, EvoAgentX integrates three MAS optimization algorithms, TextGrad, AFlow, and MIPRO, to iteratively refine agent prompts, tool configurations, and workflow topologies. We evaluate EvoAgentX on HotPotQA, MBPP, and MATH for multi-hop reasoning, code generation, and mathematical problem solving, respectively, and further assess it on real-world tasks using GAIA. Experimental results show that EvoAgentX consistently achieves significant performance improvements, including a 7.44% increase in HotPotQA F1, a 10.00% improvement in MBPP pass@1, a 10.00% gain in MATH solve accuracy, and an overall accuracy improvement of up to 20.00% on GAIA. The source code is available at: this https URL

organization_info.name: Mohamed bin Zayed University of Artificial Intelligence

keyInsights: Integrating disparate multi-agent optimization algorithms into a unified, modular framework enables dynamic, automated evolution of agentic workflows.
Iterative refinement of agent prompts, tool configurations, and workflow topologies significantly enhances task-specific performance across various domains.
Automating workflow generation and optimization reduces manual configuration burden, making sophisticated multi-agent AI systems more accessible and adaptable.
originalProblem: Multi-agent systems (MAS) often require extensive manual configuration of agent roles, task decomposition, and workflow orchestration, hindering scalability and adaptability.
Current MAS frameworks generally lack dynamic evolution and self-optimization capabilities, leading to static configurations that struggle with changing real-world conditions.
State-of-the-art multi-agent optimization techniques are fragmented and not integrated into a unified platform, complicating their practical application and comparative study.
results: On HotPotQA, TextGrad optimization increased F1 score by 7.44%, reaching 71.02% for multi-hop reasoning.
AFlow improved MBPP code generation pass@1 accuracy by 10.00% to 79.00%, and TextGrad boosted MATH problem-solving accuracy by 10.00% to 76.00%.
For real-world tasks on the GAIA benchmark, EvoAgentX achieved up to a 20.00% overall accuracy increase for existing agent systems, with one system showing a 100% gain at Level 3.
solution: EvoAgentX provides a modular, open-source framework that automates the generation, execution, and evaluation of multi-agent workflows from high-level goal descriptions.
It incorporates an "Evolving Layer" that unifies various state-of-the-art optimization algorithms, such as TextGrad, AFlow, and MIPRO, for iterative refinement.
This framework enables dynamic optimization of individual agent prompts, tool configurations, and the overall workflow topology to adapt and improve performance.
