paper_summary.summary: EvoAgentX introduces an automated open-source framework for generating, executing, and evolutionarily optimizing multi-agent workflows. It integrates diverse state-of-the-art optimization algorithms to refine agent prompts and workflow topologies, achieving substantial performance gains up to 10% on reasoning and code generation benchmarks, and up to 20% overall accuracy on the real-world GAIA multi-agent task.

title: EvoAgentX: An Automated Framework for Evolving Agentic Workflows

abstract:
Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated effectiveness in code
generation tasks. To enable LLMs to address more complex coding challenges,
existing research has focused on crafting multi-agent systems with agentic
workflows, where complex coding tasks are decomposed into sub-tasks, assigned
to specialized agents. Despite their effectiveness, current approaches heavily
rely on hand-crafted agentic workflows, with both agent topologies and prompts
manually designed, which limits their ability to automatically adapt to
different types of coding problems. To address these limitations and enable
automated workflow design, we propose \textbf{S}elf-\textbf{E}volving
\textbf{W}orkflow (\textbf{SEW}), a novel self-evolving framework that
automatically generates and optimises multi-agent workflows. Extensive
experiments on three coding benchmark datasets, including the challenging
LiveCodeBench, demonstrate that our SEW can automatically design agentic
workflows and optimise them through self-evolution, bringing up to 33\%
improvement on LiveCodeBench compared to using the backbone LLM only.
Furthermore, by investigating different representation schemes of workflow, we
provide insights into the optimal way to encode workflow information with text.

keyInsights: CoRE (Code Representation and Execution) was found to be the most effective workflow representation scheme for producing executable code, achieving a 72.7% Generation Successful Rate.
Both workflow evolution and agent evolution modules contribute to performance, with their combined application yielding up to 20.3% additional improvement over workflow evolution alone.
Hyper Evolution strategies offer greater robustness and lower variance, while Direct Evolution can achieve higher peak performance with more variability.
originalProblem: Existing multi-agent systems for LLM-based code generation rely heavily on manually crafted workflows and agent prompts, which is labor-intensive and limits adaptability.
Traditional approaches struggle with scalability, as different complex programming tasks require unique workflow structures, making bespoke design impractical.
Single-agent LLMs often fall short on complex programming problems that require multi-step reasoning, task decomposition, and collaborative problem-solving.
results: SEW consistently improved code generation performance across HumanEval, MBPP, and LiveCodeBench datasets using GPT-4o mini and Gemini-1.5-pro-002 backbone models.
On the LiveCodeBench dataset, SEW achieved a pass@1 score of 50.9% with GPT-4o mini, representing a 33.9% improvement over the standalone model's 38.0%.
Workflow evolution alone improved performance from 38.0% to 42.3% on LiveCodeBench, with additional gains from agent evolution.
solution: The SEW framework automatically generates and optimizes multi-agent workflows and individual agent prompts based on task descriptions, eliminating manual design.
It employs a Workflow Evolution Module that uses Direct Evolution operators to reconstruct and optimize workflow structural organization.
An Agent Evolution Module utilizes both Direct Evolution and Hyper Evolution operators to refine and optimize individual agent prompts within the workflows.
paper_summary.summary: The Self-Evolving Agentic Workflows (SEW) framework introduces an automated approach for generating and optimizing multi-agent systems for code generation, using evolutionary algorithms to adapt both workflow structures and agent prompts. This framework achieved a 33.9% performance improvement on the LiveCodeBench dataset with GPT-4o mini, reaching a pass@1 score of 50.9%.

title: SEW: Self-Evolving Agentic Workflows for Automated Code Generation

abstract: Autonomous agents play a crucial role in advancing Artificial General Intelligence, enabling problem decomposition and tool orchestration through Large Language Models (LLMs). However, existing paradigms face a critical trade-off. On one hand, reusable fixed workflows require manual reconfiguration upon environmental changes; on the other hand, flexible reactive loops fail to distill reasoning progress into transferable structures. We introduce Hierarchical Variable Agent (HiVA), a novel framework modeling agentic workflows as self-organized graphs with the Semantic-Topological Evolution (STEV) algorithm, which optimizes hybrid semantic-topological spaces using textual gradients as discrete-domain surrogates for backpropagation. The iterative process comprises Multi-Armed Bandit-infused forward routing, diagnostic gradient generation from environmental feedback, and coordinated updates that co-evolve individual semantics and topology for collective optimization in unknown environments. Experiments on dialogue, coding, Long-context Q&A, mathematical, and agentic benchmarks demonstrate improvements of 5-10% in task accuracy and enhanced resource efficiency over existing baselines, establishing HiVA's effectiveness in autonomous task execution.

keyInsights: Co-evolving agent semantics (what agents do) and collaborative topology (how they organize) from a singleton agent is crucial for scalable, adaptive, and self-organized intelligence.
Textual Gradients can effectively serve as a functional substitute for backpropagation in discrete, non-differentiable problem spaces, enabling LLM-driven optimization of multi-agent systems.

Leveraging the evolving multi-agent graph structure itself as a hierarchical memory allows the system to utilize historical experience at macro, meso, and micro levels for continuous self-improvement.
originalProblem: Existing LLM-based multi-agent systems face a trade-off between reusability (fixed workflows) and flexibility (reactive agent loops), struggling with continuous self-improvement and generalization.
Current methods largely treat agent coordination structures (topology) and agent behaviors (semantics) as independent, requiring manual reconfiguration or lacking dynamic self-organization.
Optimizing agentic workflows in discrete, non-differentiable spaces, which involve both dynamic agent behaviors and their evolving interconnections, remains a challenge.
results: HiVA achieved an overall average accuracy of 89.2% across diverse benchmarks, an 8.0% improvement over the Vanilla LLM baseline, demonstrating strong performance in mathematical, QA, programmatic, and textual reasoning tasks.
It significantly outperformed baselines like MaAS and AutoGPT in complex agentic environments (GAIA) with higher accuracy and a superior average Cost-efficiency Score of 5.5.

Ablation studies confirmed that Semantic Evolution and Topological Evolution were the most critical components for performance, highlighting the importance of jointly learning agent behaviors and their organizational structure.
solution: HiVA models agentic workflows as self-organized computational graphs where agents and their connections (semantics and topology) co-evolve using a Semantic-Topological Evolution (STEV) algorithm.
It introduces "Textual Gradients," derived from environmental feedback, to enable optimization in the discrete and non-differentiable hybrid space of agent parameters and graph topologies.

A Knowledge-Aware Bayesian-Bandit (KABB) routing mechanism dynamically selects task-specific execution subgraphs, balancing historical performance, task relevance, and team synergy, while an evolvable tool subsystem allows for dynamic tool synthesis and refinement.
paper_summary.summary: HiVA introduces a framework for self-organized hierarchical variable agents that co-evolves both agent semantics and collaborative topology from a single initial agent, utilizing "Textual Gradients" for optimization in discrete spaces. The system achieves an average accuracy of 89.2% across diverse benchmarks and significantly improves cost-efficiency in complex agentic environments like GAIA.

title: HiVA: Self-organized Hierarchical Variable Agent via Goal-driven Semantic-Topological Evolution

abstract:
The discovery of symbolic solutions -- mathematical expressions, logical rules, and algorithmic structures -- is fundamental to advancing scientific and engineering progress.
However, traditional methods often struggle with search efficiency and fail to integrate knowledge effectively.
While recent large language model-based (LLM-based) approaches have demonstrated improvements in search efficiency, they lack the ability to continually refine and expand upon discovered solutions and their underlying knowledge, limiting their potential for open-ended innovation.
To address these limitations, we introduce CoEvo, a novel framework that leverages large language models within an evolutionary search methodology to continually generate and refine symbolic solutions. CoEvo integrates a dynamic knowledge library, enabling open-ended innovation of solutions through effective knowledge management. Additionally, CoEvo leverages multiple representations of solutions -- including natural language, mathematical expressions, and code -- to further enhance search efficiency.
By combining the reasoning capabilities of LLMs with the exploratory power of evolutionary algorithms, CoEvo significantly improves the efficiency and scope of symbolic discovery.
Our experimental results demonstrate that this method not only enhances the efficiency of searching for symbolic solutions but also supports the ongoing discovery process, akin to human scientific endeavors. This study represents a first effort in conceptualizing the search for symbolic solutions as a lifelong, iterative
process, marking a significant step towards harnessing LLMs in the perpetual pursuit of scientific and engineering breakthroughs.
Our code is available at this https URL.
title: CoEvo: Continual Evolution of Symbolic Solutions Using Large Language Models

abstract: Large language model (LLM) agents show promise in automating machine learning (ML) engineering. However, existing agents typically operate in isolation on a given research problem, without engaging with the broader research community, where human researchers often gain insights and contribute by sharing knowledge. To bridge this gap, we introduce MLE-Live, a live evaluation framework designed to assess an agent's ability to communicate with and leverage collective knowledge from a simulated Kaggle research community. Building on this framework, we propose CoMind, a multi-agent system designed to systematically leverage external knowledge. CoMind employs an iterative parallel exploration mechanism, developing multiple solutions simultaneously to balance exploratory breadth with implementation depth. On 75 past Kaggle competitions within our MLE-Live framework, CoMind achieves a 36% medal rate, establishing a new state of the art. Critically, when deployed in eight live, ongoing competitions, CoMind outperforms 92.6% of human competitors on average, placing in the top 5% on three official leaderboards and the top 1% on one.
title: CoMind: Towards Community-Driven Agents for Machine Learning Engineering

abstract: Current Autonomous Scientific Research (ASR) systems, despite leveraging large language models (LLMs) and agentic architectures, remain constrained by fixed workflows and toolsets that prevent adaptation to evolving tasks and environments. We introduce Mimosa, an evolving multi-agent framework that automatically synthesizes task-specific multi-agent workflows and iteratively refines them through experimental feedback. Mimosa leverages the Model Context Protocol (MCP) for dynamic tool discovery, generates workflow topologies via a meta-orchestrator, executes subtasks through code-generating agents that invoke available tools and scientific software libraries, and scores executions with an LLM-based judge whose feedback drives workflow refinement. On ScienceAgentBench, Mimosa achieves a success rate of 43.1% with DeepSeek-V3.2, surpassing both single-agent baselines and static multi-agent configurations. Our results further reveal that models respond heterogeneously to multi-agent decomposition and iterative learning, indicating that the benefits of workflow evolution depend on the capabilities of the underlying execution model. Beyond these benchmarks, Mimosa modular architecture and tool-agnostic design make it readily extensible, and its fully logged execution traces and archived workflows support auditability by preserving every analytical step for inspection and potential replication. Combined with domain-expert guidance, the framework has the potential to automate a broad range of computationally accessible scientific tasks across disciplines. Released as a fully open-source platform, Mimosa aims to provide an open foundation for community-driven ASR.

keyInsights: Dynamically evolving multi-agent workflow topologies significantly enhance the adaptability and performance of autonomous scientific research systems.

Decomposing complex scientific problems into subtasks handled by specialized agents within a flexible DAG structure helps mitigate issues like semantic drift and context limitations inherent in long-horizon LLM executions.

The effectiveness of multi-agent and iterative learning approaches varies heterogeneously across different underlying language models, suggesting that optimal ASR architectures might need tailoring to specific model capabilities.
originalProblem: The scientific community faces a data-to-knowledge bottleneck and a reproducibility crisis due to fragmented methods and reporting.
Existing LLM-based AI-agent systems are limited by context constraints, information loss, semantic drift, and architectural rigidity over long-horizon tasks.
Many multi-agent systems rely on static coordination protocols, hindering their adaptation to evolving experimental demands or unexpected results.
results: DeepSeek-V3.2, when integrated with Mimosa's iterative-learning multi-agent configuration, achieved the highest success rate of 43.1% on the ScienceAgentBench benchmark.
Iterative refinement consistently improved performance for up to 8 iterations, with DeepSeek-V3.2 showing a 34% relative success rate increase from one-shot to iterative learning.
The framework enabled DeepSeek-V3.2 to achieve its highest success rate at an average cost of $1.7 per task, demonstrating a pathway towards cost-efficient scientific AI.
solution: Mimosa introduces an evolving multi-agent framework that treats workflow topology, represented as a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG), as a mutable and learnable component.
A meta-orchestrator dynamically generates initial workflows (via case-based retrieval or de novo synthesis) and iteratively refines them based on structured feedback from an LLM-as-a-judge.
The framework incorporates dynamic tool discovery and integration using the Model Context Protocol (MCP) and Toolomics, allowing agents to access and utilize distributed scientific tools.
paper_summary.summary: Mimosa, a multi-agent framework for scientific research, dynamically generates and iteratively refines workflow topologies to enhance adaptability and performance for computational scientific tasks. The framework achieved a 43.1% success rate with DeepSeek-V3.2 on the ScienceAgentBench benchmark, demonstrating improved capabilities over static agent systems.

title: Mimosa Framework: Toward Evolving Multi-Agent Systems for Scientific Research

abstract: Large Language Model (LLM) based agents are powerful yet fundamentally static after deployment, lacking the ability to autonomously expand capabilities, generate new tools, or evolve their reasoning. This work introduces a hierarchical self-evolving multi-agent framework that integrates a Base LLM, an operational SLM agent, a Code-Generation LLM, and a Teacher-LLM to enable continuous adaptation. The workflow begins with the agent attempting a task using reasoning and existing tools; if unsuccessful, it escalates to tool synthesis through the Code-Gen LLM, and when failures persist, it triggers an evolution phase using Curriculum Learning (CL), Reward-Based Learning (RL), or Genetic Algorithm (GA) evolution. Using the TaskCraft dataset rich in hierarchical tasks, tool-use traces, and difficulty scaling we evaluate these paradigms. CL delivers fast recovery and strong generalization, RL excels on high-difficulty tasks, and GA offers high behavioral diversity. Across all settings, evolved agents outperform their originals, demonstrating robust, autonomous, self-improving agentic evolution.

keyInsights: Integrating dynamic tool generation and adaptive learning paradigms into a hierarchical multi-agent system provides a pragmatic approach to overcoming the staticity of current LLM agents and advancing towards Artificial General Intelligence.

A multi-faceted approach, leveraging the distinct strengths of Curriculum Learning for efficiency and generalization, Reward-Based Learning for high-difficulty task mastery, and Genetic Algorithms for exploration and diversity, yields comprehensive self-improvement.
Structured feedback loops, verifiable performance metrics, and systematic agent lifecycle management are crucial for enabling scalable, robust, and continuously improving autonomous AI systems.
originalProblem: Existing Large Language Model (LLM)-based agents are static after deployment, operating with fixed architectures, prompts, and workflows.
This static nature severely limits their ability to adapt to novel tasks, dynamic environments, or evolving knowledge distributions.
Current agents lack mechanisms for autonomous capability expansion, reasoning evolution, and continuous improvement, hindering their long-term autonomy and robustness.
results: Evolved agents across all learning paradigms (Curriculum Learning, Reward-Based Learning, Genetic Algorithms) consistently and significantly outperformed their original, static counterparts in task success rates on the TaskCraft dataset.
Curriculum Learning demonstrated the highest overall learning efficiency, fastest convergence, and strongest generalization across varying task difficulties, showing the most efficient tool usage.

Reward-Based Learning achieved superior performance on high-difficulty tasks, and Genetic Algorithms exhibited the greatest behavioral diversity and occasional large, rapid performance jumps across generations.
solution: A hierarchical multi-agent framework orchestrates a Base LLM, Operational Agent, Code-Generation LLM, and Teacher-LLM for continuous adaptation and improvement.
The system implements an escalation model: agents first attempt a task, then a Code-Generation LLM synthesizes new tools if needed, and finally, an evolution phase is triggered if failures persist.

The evolution phase utilizes Curriculum Learning, Reward-Based Learning, or Genetic Algorithms to train cloned agents, which are then rigorously evaluated and promoted to replace predecessors if performance improves significantly.
paper_summary.summary: Rastrai researchers developed a hierarchical framework for self-evolving multi-agent Large Language Model systems, enabling agents to autonomously adapt, generate new tools, and refine reasoning. Evaluations using the TaskCraft dataset showed that agents evolved through curriculum learning, reward-based learning, or genetic algorithms consistently surpassed static agents in task success rates, tool efficiency, and overall performance.

title: Towards AGI A Pragmatic Approach Towards Self Evolving Agent

abstract: Scaffolding Large Language Models (LLMs) into multi-agent systems often improves performance on complex tasks, but the safety impact of such scaffolds has not been thoroughly explored. We introduce AgentBreeder, a framework for multi-objective self-improving evolutionary search over scaffolds. We evaluate discovered scaffolds on widely recognized reasoning, mathematics, and safety benchmarks and compare them with popular baselines. In "blue" mode, we see a 79.4% average uplift in safety benchmark performance while maintaining or improving capability scores. In "red" mode, we find adversarially weak scaffolds emerging concurrently with capability optimization. Our work demonstrates the risks of multi-agent scaffolding and provides a framework for mitigating them. Code is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: High task capability in multi-agent systems can coexist with significant safety vulnerabilities, indicating "outer misalignment" where the scaffold design promotes harmful behaviors.
"Red team" scaffolds designed to exploit safety weaknesses are discovered more efficiently and with less effort than "blue team" scaffolds aimed at enhancing safety.

Multi-objective optimization for both capability and safety leads to superior overall solutions compared to single-objective optimization, and reveals a tendency for "reward hacking" in safety metrics.
originalProblem: Multi-agent large language model (LLM) systems, or "scaffolds," introduce novel and poorly understood AI safety risks not covered by single-agent safety research.
Existing AI alignment methods primarily address "unipolar scenarios," failing to evaluate the complex "multi-polar challenges" arising from interconnected LLM agents.
A critical gap exists in systematically identifying and mitigating vulnerabilities in these increasingly autonomous and interactive AI systems.
results: The BLUEAGENTBREEDER mode significantly improved safety performance, achieving an average 79.4% uplift and a maximum 110.7% increase on the SaladData benchmark without sacrificing capability.

The REDAGENTBREEDER mode efficiently identified unsafe scaffolds, with the best performing one reaching 81.6% accuracy on an inverted safety metric, requiring only half the evolutionary budget of the defense mode.

Multi-objective optimization for both capability and safety proved more effective than single-objective approaches, yielding better overall solutions and exposing a "reward hacking" phenomenon where systems produced unhelpful but safe responses.
solution: An evolutionary, open-ended framework named AgentBreeder was developed, designed to generate and explore a diverse population of multi-agent LLM scaffolds.
The framework employs multi-objective optimization, allowing for the concurrent evolution of scaffolds that balance both task capability and safety adherence.
AgentBreeder operates in "red team" mode to discover vulnerable architectures and "blue team" mode to engineer robust and safer multi-agent systems.
paper_summary.summary: An evolutionary framework called AgentBreeder was developed to systematically identify and mitigate safety risks in multi-agent large language model (LLM) systems. This framework achieved an average 79.4% increase in safety benchmark performance, while also demonstrating that unsafe multi-agent configurations are more readily discoverable.

title: AgentBreeder: Mitigating the AI Safety Risks of Multi-Agent Scaffolds via Self-Improvement

abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have made significant progress in handling complex programming tasks. However, current methods rely on manual model selection and fixed workflows, which limit their ability to adapt to changing task complexities. To address this, we propose SEMAG, a Self-Evolutionary Multi-Agent code Generation framework that mimics human coding practices. It decomposes programming tasks into stages, including planning, coding, debugging, and discussion, while adapting workflows to task difficulty. Its self-evolutionary agents can access the latest models in real time and automatically upgrade the backbone model. SEMAG sets new state-of-the-art Pass@1 accuracy across benchmarks. Using identical backbone models, SEMAG outperforms prior methods by 3.3% on CodeContests. When augmented with self-evolutionary model selection that automatically identifies optimal backbones, SEMAG reaches 52.6%, showcasing both framework effectiveness and adaptability to evolving LLM capabilities.

keyInsights: Dynamically adjusting the reasoning depth and employing a multi-agent collaborative refinement process, including discussion and decision modules, significantly improves code generation performance and allows escaping local minima.
Automated self-evolution agents can effectively identify and deploy optimal, evolving backbone LLMs in real-time, enabling continuous adaptation and performance enhancement.
Trace-guided debugging combined with semantic analysis and targeted suggestions is crucial for effective iterative code refinement, leading to more robust solutions.
originalProblem: Existing multi-agent code generation frameworks often use fixed reasoning depths, leading to inefficiency for simple tasks and insufficient reasoning for complex ones.
Many debugging pipelines employ only a single iteration, which can lead to local minima when initial code output is significantly flawed.
Current LLM-based code generation systems are tightly coupled to a single static backbone model, limiting their adaptability to evolving LLM capabilities or changing task characteristics.
results: SEMAG achieved new state-of-the-art Pass@1 accuracy on all seven benchmarks, including 98.8% on HumanEval (GPT-4o) and improvements of 1.8-6.0% over previous best baselines (LPW) on foundational benchmarks.
The self-evolution mechanism successfully identified Claude-3.7-Sonnet as a superior backbone for the CodeContests benchmark, achieving 52.6% Pass@1 accuracy compared to 38.0% with GPT-4o.

The hierarchical prompting strategy reduced token consumption by 19.3% on HumanEval and 15.5% on MBPP compared to LPW, demonstrating improved resource efficiency for simpler tasks while maintaining or improving accuracy.
solution: SEMAG implements a four-level hierarchical code synthesis framework that progressively increases computational effort based on task complexity, starting from direct generation to multi-agent collaborative refinement.
An adaptive level transition mechanism, based on execution trace similarity, dynamically adjusts the reasoning depth, ensuring flexible resource allocation rather than fixed iteration thresholds.

A self-evolution mechanism uses parallel selector agents to autonomously identify and select the most suitable, up-to-date backbone LLMs for specific tasks by evaluating candidates based on sampled performance and reaching consensus.
paper_summary.summary: A hierarchical multi-agent framework named SEMAG is introduced for code generation, dynamically adapting reasoning depth and selecting optimal large language models. It achieved state-of-the-art Pass@1 accuracy across seven benchmarks, with improvements up to 6.8% over previous baselines on MBPP-ET (GPT-3.5) and 5.7% on competition-level tasks (GPT-4o), demonstrating enhanced resource efficiency and adaptability.

title: SEMAG: Self-Evolutionary Multi-Agent Code Generation

abstract: LLM-driven evolutionary systems have shown promise for automated science discovery, yet existing approaches such as AlphaEvolve rely on full-code histories that are context-inefficient and potentially provide weak evolutionary guidance. In this work, we first formalize the evolutionary agents as a general Expectation-Maximization framework, where the language model samples candidate programs (E-step) and the system updates the control context based on evaluation feedback (M-step). Under this view, constructing context via full-code snapshots constitutes a suboptimal M-step, as redundant implement details dilutes core algorithmic ideas, making it difficult to provide clear inspirations for evolution. To address this, we propose DeltaEvolve, a momentum-driven evolutionary framework that replaces full-code history with structured semantic delta capturing how and why modifications between successive nodes affect performance. As programs are often decomposable, semantic delta usually contains many effective components which are transferable and more informative to drive improvement. By organizing semantic delta through multi-level database and progressive disclosure mechanism, input tokens are further reduced. Empirical evaluations on tasks across diverse scientific domains show that our framework can discover better solution with less token consumption over full-code-based evolutionary agents.

keyInsights: LLM-driven evolutionary agents primarily learn from patterns within selected high-quality solutions in their context, rather than implicitly regressing against explicit numerical scalar feedback.
Formalizing LLM-driven evolution within an Expectation-Maximization framework highlights that the "M-step" (context update) is crucial for guiding optimization, analogous to a gradient update.

Semantic modifications (deltas) are more informative and transferable for guiding future program updates than static, full-code representations of solutions, providing stronger evolutionary signals within limited context budgets.
originalProblem: Existing LLM-driven evolutionary agents suffer from high computational costs and latency due to extensive use of full-code histories in the context window, leading to a "token bottleneck."

Providing LLMs with full code snapshots offers weak evolutionary guidance, as core algorithmic ideas are often obscured by implementation details, making it difficult to discern performance-affecting changes.
Current methods fail to provide objective-aware context construction, limiting the effective utilization of historical information for long-horizon reasoning.
results: DeltaEvolve achieved solution quality comparable to or superior to all baseline methods, including AlphaEvolve, across five diverse scientific domains like black-box optimization and symbolic regression.
The framework demonstrated a substantial reduction in total token consumption, averaging approximately 36.79% less than the state-of-the-art AlphaEvolve baseline across all evaluated tasks.

Ablation studies confirmed that context selection (presenting high-quality examples) is a more critical driver of LLM performance than explicit numerical scalar feedback, validating the focus on structured, qualitative guidance.
solution: Introduces "semantic deltas" as a momentum-driven evolutionary guidance mechanism, capturing interpretable logic changes and qualitative performance shifts between successive program versions.
Implements a multi-level database to store information at varying abstraction levels: concise delta summaries, detailed delta plans with hypotheses, and the full executable code.

Utilizes a progressive disclosure sampler that dynamically renders context, presenting older elite solutions as summaries, recent inspirations as detailed plans, and the current parent program as full code to optimize token usage while providing relevant guidance.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at The Ohio State University and Microsoft developed DeltaEvolve, a framework for LLM-driven evolutionary systems that replaces full-code histories with structured semantic deltas and employs a progressive disclosure sampler. This approach achieves solution quality comparable to or superior to prior methods while reducing total token consumption by approximately 36.79% across five diverse scientific discovery tasks.

title: DeltaEvolve: Accelerating Scientific Discovery through Momentum-Driven Evolution

abstract: Recent advancements in large language models (LLMs) have brought significant changes to various domains, especially through LLM-driven autonomous agents. A representative scenario is in software development, where LLM agents demonstrate efficient collaboration, task division, and assurance of software quality, markedly reducing the need for manual involvement. However, these agents frequently perform a variety of tasks independently, without benefiting from past experiences, which leads to repeated mistakes and inefficient attempts in multi-step task execution. To this end, we introduce Experiential Co-Learning, a novel LLM-agent learning framework in which instructor and assistant agents gather shortcut-oriented experiences from their historical trajectories and use these past experiences for future task execution. The extensive experiments demonstrate that the framework enables agents to tackle unseen software-developing tasks more effectively. We anticipate that our insights will guide LLM agents towards enhanced autonomy and contribute to their evolutionary growth in cooperative learning. The code and data are available at this https URL.

author_info.institution: King's College London, University of London

organization_info.name: Beijing University of Posts and Telecommunications

keyInsights: Intelligently extracted "shortcuts" from collaborative trajectories, which skip redundant steps, are more effective for cross-task learning than experiences derived from simple sequential successful steps.

Both instructor and assistant agents must accumulate and leverage experiences for optimal performance, with the assistant's experience proving particularly impactful for generating effective solutions.
The deduplication process of task execution graphs is vital for extracting high-quality, non-redundant experiences, enabling efficient and accurate learning.
originalProblem: Current LLM-driven multi-agent collaboration frameworks often treat each task independently, lacking a mechanism to systematically learn from past successes and failures across different tasks.
Agents frequently commit repetitive errors and engage in inefficient trial-and-error processes, leading to prolonged execution times and increased human intervention.
Existing multi-agent systems lack the ability to accumulate and leverage cross-task experience, leading to a "stateless" operation across diverse problem sets.
results: The Co-Learning framework achieved a Quality score of 0.7304, substantially outperforming previous methods like ChatDev (0.4267) in software completeness and executability.
Demonstrated improved efficiency, completing tasks in 122.775 seconds on average, faster than other multi-agent baselines such as ChatDev (148.215s) and MetaGPT (154.0s).
Consistently showed higher win rates in both GPT-4 (77.25% vs. ChatDev) and human evaluations (91.41% vs. ChatDev), confirming the subjective and objective superiority of the generated software.
solution: Introduces "Experiential Co-Learning," a multi-agent learning paradigm for software development that enables agents to gather and utilize useful experiences from historical communication.
Employs a Co-Tracking module to capture procedural trajectories of collaborative task execution, formalized as a chain graph of solutions and instructions.

Features a Co-Memorizing module that extracts "shortcut-oriented experiences" from deduplicated task execution graphs by identifying non-adjacent nodes with significant information gain, generating pseudo-instructions for these shortcuts.

Utilizes a Co-Reasoning module to apply accumulated experiences via retrieval-augmented generation, allowing both instructor and assistant agents to generate experience-enhanced instructions and solutions for new tasks.
paper_summary.summary: Tsinghua University researchers developed "Experiential Co-Learning," a multi-agent framework that enables LLM agents to accumulate and utilize "shortcut-oriented experiences" from past software development tasks, leading to a Quality score of 0.7304 for generated software and reduced execution time compared to previous methods.

title: Experiential Co-Learning of Software-Developing Agents

abstract: Recent advancements have increasingly focused on leveraging large language models (LLMs) to construct autonomous agents for complex problem-solving tasks. However, existing approaches predominantly employ a single-agent framework to generate search branches and estimate rewards during Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) planning. This single-agent paradigm inherently limits exploration capabilities, often resulting in insufficient diversity among generated branches and suboptimal planning performance. To overcome these limitations, we propose Synergistic Multi-agent Planning with Heterogeneous langauge model assembly (SYMPHONY), a novel multi-agent planning framework that integrates a pool of heterogeneous language model-based agents. By leveraging diverse reasoning patterns across agents, SYMPHONY enhances rollout diversity and facilitates more effective exploration. Empirical results across multiple benchmark tasks show that SYMPHONY achieves strong performance even when instantiated with open-source LLMs deployable on consumer-grade hardware. When enhanced with cloud-based LLMs accessible via API, SYMPHONY demonstrates further improvements, outperforming existing state-of-the-art baselines and underscoring the effectiveness of heterogeneous multi-agent coordination in planning tasks.

keyInsights: Employing heterogeneous LLMs dramatically increases search branch diversity, which directly correlates with significant improvements in task performance.

Adaptive UCB-based agent scheduling effectively balances exploration and exploitation among diverse agents, leading to robust planning without the overhead of uniform agent design or complex coordination.
Decentralized reflection and shared memory enable agents to collectively learn from past failures and continuously adapt their behavior in a lightweight and scalable manner.
Uncertainty-aware evaluation, specifically EMCS, stabilizes MCTS planning by appropriately penalizing ambiguous outcomes, thereby leading to more reliable decision-making.
originalProblem: Single LLM-based planning in Monte Carlo Tree Search often suffers from limited exploration diversity, producing highly similar outputs and hindering the discovery of optimal solutions.
Existing LLM-based autonomous agents are prone to suboptimal planning performance due to narrow search trajectories, making them susceptible to local optima in complex tasks.
Current methods exhibit computational inefficiency and high token consumption, as a constricted search space necessitates excessive sampling for minor variations of similar ideas.
results: Achieved state-of-the-art performance on complex tasks, including 0.79 Exact Match on HotpotQA, 0.88 average score on WebShop (exceeding human experts), and high pass rates (0.965 Python, 0.974 Rust) on MBPP code generation.

Demonstrated enhanced exploration diversity, with heterogeneous agents producing over 80% 4-Unique expansions in contrast to less than 20% for single-agent methods, leading to over 30% performance gains on MBPP.

Improved planning efficiency and cost-effectiveness by requiring fewer node expansions and reducing token consumption by up to 95% (e.g., 7,906 tokens per question on HotpotQA) while maintaining higher accuracy.
solution: Developed a heterogeneous agent pool comprising diverse LLMs, selected for varied pretraining, architectures, and reasoning styles, to ensure distinct inductive biases for MCTS expansions.

Implemented adaptive agent scheduling via a UCB (Upper Confidence Bound) strategy, dynamically allocating agents based on their historical effectiveness for generating actions, evaluations, and reflections.

Introduced pool-wise memory sharing where natural language reflections from unsuccessful trajectories are broadcast and incorporated into all agents' prompts, enabling continuous, lightweight adaptation without explicit model retraining.
Integrated Entropy-Modulated Confidence Scoring (EMCS) into node evaluation, adjusting rewards by penalizing uncertain predictions to ensure stable and reliable MCTS planning.
paper_summary.summary: A multi-agent planning framework named SYMPHONY integrates heterogeneous large language models (LLMs) with Monte Carlo Tree Search to enhance exploration diversity. It achieves state-of-the-art performance on HotpotQA (0.79 Exact Match) and WebShop (0.88 average score), demonstrating up to a 95% reduction in token consumption compared to existing methods.

title: SYMPHONY: Synergistic Multi-agent Planning with Heterogeneous Language Model Assembly

abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are evolving at an unprecedented pace and have exhibited considerable capability in the realm of natural language processing (NLP) with world knowledge. Benefiting from ultra-large-scale training corpora, a single LLM can manage typical NLP tasks competently. However, its performance in executing reasoning tasks is still confined by the limitations of its internal representations. To push this boundary further, we introduce Corex in this paper, a suite of novel general-purpose strategies that transform LLMs into autonomous agents pioneering multi-model collaborations for complex task-solving. Inspired by human behaviors, Corex is constituted by diverse collaboration paradigms including Debate, Review, and Retrieve modes, which collectively work towards enhancing the factuality, faithfulness, and reliability of the reasoning process. These paradigms foster task-agnostic approaches that enable LLMs to ''think outside the box,'' thereby overcoming hallucinations and providing better solutions. Through extensive experiments across four different types of reasoning tasks, we demonstrate that orchestrating multiple LLMs to work in concert yields substantially better performance compared to existing methods. Further results and in-depth analysis demonstrate the cost-effectiveness of our method, facilitating collaboration among different LLMs and promoting annotation efficiency.

keyInsights: Multi-model collaboration via human-inspired strategies (Discuss, Review, Retrieve) effectively overcomes limitations of single-model reasoning, such as cumulative errors and buggy code.
These collaborative modes are complementary and can be combined for further performance gains, particularly in challenging numerical tasks.

The framework offers substantial cost-effectiveness, achieving comparable or superior performance with significantly fewer tokens compared to majority voting methods, and enables weaker open-source models to collaborate with stronger models for enhanced capabilities.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) struggle with complex, multi-step reasoning despite their proficiency in general NLP tasks.
Existing reasoning techniques like Chain-of-Thought (CoT) and Program-Aided Language Models (PAL) rely on static internal representations, leading to cumulative errors, hallucinations, or buggy code.
Prior multi-model approaches often incur high computational costs (e.g., extensive sampling) or have limited mechanisms for genuine error correction and self-refinement.
results: Corex consistently outperforms strong baselines like CoT, CoT-SC(10), ComplexCoT, and PAL/PoT across 18 diverse mathematical, commonsense, symbolic, and semi-structured reasoning tasks.
Corex-Review_Code achieves 63.6% accuracy on GSM-Hard, surpassing PAL/PoT (62.1%) and CoT-SC(10) (45.2%), indicating its efficacy in fixing and generating correct code.
Corex significantly reduces computational costs, typically consuming 5-10% of tokens compared to majority voting methods while achieving superior or comparable performance.
solution: Corex transforms LLMs into autonomous agents that engage in multi-model collaboration through three human-inspired paradigms: Discuss, Review, and Retrieve.
The 'Discuss' mode organizes agents into teams and a judge for iterative debates, aiming to enhance the factuality and diversity of rationales without requiring full consensus.

The 'Review' mode employs sequential peer review where agents refine a primary agent's solution, directly targeting cumulative errors in reasoning chains and bugs in generated code, while the 'Retrieve' mode selects the most faithful answer from a candidate pool by evaluating the alignment between reasoning and prediction.
paper_summary.summary: Corex developed a multi-model collaboration framework for Large Language Models, utilizing human-inspired 'Discuss,' 'Review,' and 'Retrieve' strategies. This approach enhances complex reasoning across mathematical, commonsense, symbolic, and semi-structured tasks, often outperforming existing methods while significantly reducing computational costs.

title: Corex: Pushing the Boundaries of Complex Reasoning through Multi-Model Collaboration

abstract: Evolve-based agent such as AlphaEvolve is one of the notable successes in using Large Language Models (LLMs) to build AI Scientists. These agents tackle open-ended scientific problems by iteratively improving and evolving programs, leveraging the prior knowledge and reasoning capabilities of LLMs. Despite the success, existing evolve-based agents lack targeted guidance for evolution and effective mechanisms for organizing and utilizing knowledge acquired from past evolutionary experience. Consequently, they suffer from decreasing evolution efficiency and exhibit oscillatory behavior when approaching known performance boundaries. To mitigate the gap, we develop CausalEvolve, equipped with a causal scratchpad that leverages LLMs to identify and reason about guiding factors for evolution. At the beginning, CausalEvolve first identifies outcome-level factors that offer complementary inspirations in improving the target objective. During the evolution, CausalEvolve also inspects surprise patterns during the evolution and abductive reasoning to hypothesize new factors, which in turn offer novel directions. Through comprehensive experiments, we show that CausalEvolve effectively improves the evolutionary efficiency and discovers better solutions in 4 challenging open-ended scientific tasks.
title: CausalEvolve: Towards Open-Ended Discovery with Causal Scratchpad

abstract: Large language models are transforming systems research by automating the discovery of performance-critical algorithms for computer systems. Despite plausible codes generated by LLMs, producing solutions that meet the stringent correctness and performance requirements of systems demands iterative optimization. Test-time reinforcement learning offers high search efficiency but requires parameter updates infeasible under API-only access, while existing training-free evolutionary methods suffer from inefficient context utilization and undirected search. We introduce ContextEvolve, a multi-agent framework that achieves RL-level search efficiency under strict parameter-blind constraints by decomposing optimization context into three orthogonal dimensions: a Summarizer Agent condenses semantic state via code-to-language abstraction, a Navigator Agent distills optimization direction from trajectory analysis, and a Sampler Agent curates experience distribution through prioritized exemplar retrieval. This orchestration forms a functional isomorphism with RL-mapping to state representation, policy gradient, and experience replay-enabling principled optimization in a textual latent space. On the ADRS benchmark, ContextEvolve outperforms state-of-the-art baselines by 33.3% while reducing token consumption by 29.0%. Codes for our work are released at this https URL

keyInsights: Decomposing and compressing the optimization context into distinct, semantically rich dimensions via specialized agents effectively mitigates context window limitations and information bloat in LLM-driven iterative processes.
Achieving RL-level search efficiency in a training-free, parameter-blind setting is possible by functionally emulating RL mechanisms through multi-agent interactions in a textual latent space.

Directing LLM-based code evolution benefits more from abstract, high-level guidance and semantically informative experience sampling (even from lower-scoring or failed attempts) than from overly specific instructions or solely high-performing examples.
originalProblem: Existing LLM-based iterative optimization methods face limitations due to the computational cost of test-time reinforcement learning and the inability to update LLM parameters with API-only access.
Training-free evolutionary approaches suffer from low search efficiency caused by inefficient context utilization (information bloat) and undirected exploration within the LLM's context window.
The strict correctness and performance demands of systems code require robust optimization techniques that consistently surpass raw LLM outputs and human-engineered baselines.
results: ContextEvolve consistently outperformed state-of-the-art baselines across five ADRS benchmarks, yielding an average score improvement of 33.3% and updating the best-so-far solution 83.3% more frequently.

The framework achieved an average reduction in token consumption by 17.3% compared to existing evolutionary methods, by curating fewer, high-value exemplars and increasing information density per token.

In the Load Balancing task, ContextEvolve discovered algorithmic breakthroughs, such as a 'largest remainder proportional apportionment strategy,' boosting balancedness by over 36% compared to baselines.
solution: ContextEvolve introduces a multi-agent framework comprising a Summarizer, Navigator, and Sampler, which collectively compress raw interaction logs into a semantically rich optimization context.

The framework establishes a functional isomorphism with core Reinforcement Learning (RL) concepts, including state representation, policy gradient, and experience replay, operating entirely within a textual latent space.

Specialized agents manage orthogonal dimensions of the optimization context: the Summarizer condenses semantic states, the Navigator distills optimization directions, and the Sampler curates informative few-shot exemplars for the Generator LLM.
paper_summary.summary: ContextEvolve presents a multi-agent framework designed for efficient iterative optimization of systems code using Large Language Models under API-only access constraints. The framework achieves an average score improvement of 33.3% over state-of-the-art baselines and reduces token consumption by 17.3% while frequently updating the best-so-far solution 83.3% more often.

title: ContextEvolve: Multi-Agent Context Compression for Systems Code Optimization

abstract: Large language model (LLM) agents have shown increasing promise for collaborative task completion. However, existing multi-agent frameworks often rely on static workflows, fixed roles, and limited inter-agent communication, reducing their effectiveness in open-ended, high-complexity domains. This paper proposes a coordination framework that enables adaptiveness through three core mechanisms: dynamic task routing, bidirectional feedback, and parallel agent evaluation. The framework allows agents to reallocate tasks based on confidence and workload, exchange structured critiques to iteratively improve outputs, and crucially compete on high-ambiguity subtasks with evaluator-driven selection of the most suitable result. We instantiate these principles in a modular architecture and demonstrate substantial improvements in factual coverage, coherence, and efficiency over static and partially adaptive baselines. Our findings highlight the benefits of incorporating both adaptiveness and structured competition in multi-agent LLM systems.
title: Parallelism Meets Adaptiveness: Scalable Documents Understanding in Multi-Agent LLM Systems

abstract: Automatic Heuristic Design (AHD) has gained traction as a promising solution for solving combinatorial optimization problems (COPs). Large Language Models (LLMs) have emerged and become a promising approach to achieving AHD, but current LLM-based AHD research often only considers a single role. This paper proposes RoCo, a novel Multi-Agent Role-Based System, to enhance the diversity and quality of AHD through multi-role collaboration. RoCo coordinates four specialized LLM-guided agents-explorer, exploiter, critic, and integrator-to collaboratively generate high-quality heuristics. The explorer promotes long-term potential through creative, diversity-driven thinking, while the exploiter focuses on short-term improvements via conservative, efficiency-oriented refinements. The critic evaluates the effectiveness of each evolution step and provides targeted feedback and reflection. The integrator synthesizes proposals from the explorer and exploiter, balancing innovation and exploitation to drive overall progress. These agents interact in a structured multi-round process involving feedback, refinement, and elite mutations guided by both short-term and accumulated long-term reflections. We evaluate RoCo on five different COPs under both white-box and black-box settings. Experimental results demonstrate that RoCo achieves superior performance, consistently generating competitive heuristics that outperform existing methods including ReEvo and HSEvo, both in white-box and black-box scenarios. This role-based collaborative paradigm establishes a new standard for robust and high-performing AHD.
title: RoCo: Role-Based LLMs Collaboration for Automatic Heuristic Design

abstract:
Large Language Model-based multi-agent systems (MAS) have shown remarkable
progress in solving complex tasks through collaborative reasoning and
inter-agent critique. However, existing approaches typically treat each task in
isolation, resulting in redundant computations and limited generalization
across structurally similar tasks. To address this, we introduce multi-agent
cross-task experiential learning (MAEL), a novel framework that endows
LLM-driven agents with explicit cross-task learning and experience
accumulation. We model the task-solving workflow on a graph-structured
multi-agent collaboration network, where agents propagate information and
coordinate via explicit connectivity. During the experiential learning phase,
we quantify the quality for each step in the task-solving workflow and store
the resulting rewards along with the corresponding inputs and outputs into each
agent's individual experience pool. During inference, agents retrieve
high-reward, task-relevant experiences as few-shot examples to enhance the
effectiveness of each reasoning step, thereby enabling more accurate and
efficient multi-agent collaboration. Experimental results on diverse datasets
demonstrate that MAEL empowers agents to learn from prior task experiences
effectively-achieving faster convergence and producing higher-quality solutions
on current tasks.

organization_info.name: Beijing University of Posts and Telecommunications

keyInsights: Quantifying the quality of each reasoning step with rewards and storing these as experiences enables agents to learn from and transfer knowledge across tasks, enhancing efficiency and solution quality.
Integrating experience retrieval allows MAS to move beyond myopic, scratch-based problem-solving towards an adaptive, experience-driven paradigm.

Localized optimization at individual decision steps through step-wise experience retrieval contributes most to overall performance, suggesting that diverse, context-specific references promote modular and effective problem-solving.
originalProblem: Existing LLM-based multi-agent systems (MAS) treat each task in isolation, solving problems from scratch without leveraging past experiences.
This isolated approach leads to redundant computations, suboptimal sample efficiency, and prolonged convergence times, especially for structurally similar tasks.
A lack of explicit cross-task learning limits agents' generalization capabilities and prevents the transfer of insights, hindering the development of robust multi-agent intelligence.
results: MAEL with task-wise experience retrieval improved performance by an average of 4.02 percentage points over the MAEL framework without experience, and 4.89 percentage points over GPT-4o-mini.

Experience integration significantly reduced token usage (e.g., 49% on CommonGen-Hard) and convergence rounds (e.g., 26% on SRDD), while boosting quality (e.g., 12.04 percentage points on CommonGen-Hard).
Combining high-reward and high-similarity experiences yielded the largest gains, increasing performance by 12.5% on HumanEval and 10.1% on CommonGen compared to no-experience baselines.
solution: MAEL introduces an explicit experiential learning phase for continuous cross-task experience accumulation and a reward-guided experience retrieval mechanism.
The framework models MAS as a graph where LLM agents have individual experience pools, utilizing a divide-and-conquer strategy with solver-critique collaboration.
During inference, agents retrieve task-relevant, high-reward experiences from their pools as few-shot examples to guide decision-making at each reasoning step.
paper_summary.summary: The Multi-Agent Cross-Task Experiential Learning (MAEL) framework improves LLM-based multi-agent system performance by enabling agents to accumulate and retrieve high-reward experiences from past tasks. This approach leads to more efficient and higher-quality solutions, demonstrating consistent improvements in solution quality, token consumption, and convergence iterations across various benchmarks.

title: Cross-Task Experiential Learning on LLM-based Multi-Agent Collaboration

abstract: Multi-objective optimization problems (MOPs) are ubiquitous in real-world applications, presenting a complex challenge of balancing multiple conflicting objectives. Traditional evolutionary algorithms (EAs), though effective, often rely on domain-specific expertise and iterative fine-tuning, hindering adaptability to unseen MOPs. In recent years, the advent of Large Language Models (LLMs) has revolutionized software engineering by enabling the autonomous generation and refinement of programs. Leveraging this breakthrough, we propose a new LLM-based framework that autonomously designs EA operators for solving MOPs. The proposed framework includes a robust testing module to refine the generated EA operator through error-driven dialogue with LLMs, a dynamic selection strategy along with informative prompting-based crossover and mutation to fit textual optimization pipeline. Our approach facilitates the design of EA operators without the extensive demands for expert intervention, thereby speeding up the innovation of EA operators. Empirical studies across various MOP categories validate the robustness and superior performance of our proposed framework.

keyInsights: LLMs, when guided by sophisticated feedback and evolutionary mechanisms, can autonomously create and iteratively improve complex optimization algorithms that are competitive with, or superior to, human-designed ones.
The "Pilot Run & Repair" mechanism is crucial for handling errors inherent in LLM-generated code, enabling the autonomous debugging and refinement necessary for functional algorithmic design.

Dynamic selection strategies and problem-specific context within prompts are vital for fostering effective adaptation and preventing stagnation in LLM-based operator evolution for diverse optimization problems.
originalProblem: Designing effective Evolutionary Algorithm (EA) operators for Multi-objective Optimization Problems (MOPs) traditionally requires extensive domain-specific expertise, manual tuning, and laborious trial-and-error.

Existing Large Language Model (LLM)-based program evolution methods often suffer from limitations such as overly succinct prompts, unsuitable evolutionary strategies for textual optimization, and a lack of robust error handling.
There was a lack of substantial research specifically on leveraging LLMs to autonomously develop and refine *innovative EA operators* tailored for the complexities of MOPs.
results: LLMOPT-evolved operators achieved the lowest average Inverted Generational Distance (IGD) on testing Continuous MOPs, outperforming all compared human-engineered methods in challenging instances like DTLZ-1, DTLZ-3, DTLZ-6, and DTLZ-7.

For Multi-objective Knapsack Problems, the LLMOPT-generated EA operator consistently outperformed all ten competing human-designed methods, demonstrating faster convergence and superior Hypervolume values.

The framework's operator for Multi-objective Traveling Salesman Problems secured the highest overall averaged Hypervolume value, outperforming most algorithms despite having a higher running time, indicating superior solution quality.
solution: The LLMOPT framework introduces an iterative evolutionary process where LLMs generate, crossover, and mutate EA operators using meticulously crafted, informative prompts.
A robust "Pilot Run & Repair" module is integrated, which uses compilation and runtime errors as dialogue-based feedback to the LLM for autonomous code refinement and error correction.

Tailored textual evolutionary strategies, including a dynamic selection mechanism and informative crossover/mutation prompts, are employed to enhance exploration, maintain diversity, and prevent stagnation in the LLM-driven search.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from The Hong Kong Polytechnic University and Chongqing University developed LLMOPT, an autonomous framework that utilizes Large Language Models to design and iteratively refine Evolutionary Algorithm operators for Multi-objective Optimization Problems. This system produces operators that achieve competitive or superior performance against state-of-the-art human-engineered algorithms across diverse continuous and combinatorial MOPs, featuring an integrated error-driven feedback mechanism.

title: Autonomous Multi-Objective Optimization Using Large Language Model

abstract:
Agents powered by advanced large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated
impressive capabilities across diverse complex applications. Recently,
Multi-Agent Systems (MAS), wherein multiple agents collaborate and communicate
with each other, have exhibited enhanced capabilities in complex tasks, such as
high-quality code generation and arithmetic reasoning. However, the development
of such systems often relies on handcrafted methods, and the literature on
systematic design and optimization of LLM-based MAS remains limited.
In this work, we introduce OMAC, a general framework designed for holistic
optimization of LLM-based MAS. Specifically, we identify five key optimization
dimensions for MAS, encompassing both agent functionality and collaboration
structure. Building upon these dimensions, we first propose a general
algorithm, utilizing two actors termed the Semantic Initializer and the
Contrastive Comparator, to optimize any single dimension. Then, we present an
algorithm for joint optimization across multiple dimensions. Extensive
experiments demonstrate the superior performance of OMAC on code generation,
arithmetic reasoning, and general reasoning tasks against state-of-the-art
approaches.

organization_info.name: The University of Texas at Austin

title: OMAC: A Broad Optimization Framework for LLM-Based Multi-Agent  Collaboration

abstract: Large Language Model (LLM) based multi-agent systems (MAS) have shown promise in tackling complex tasks, but often rely on predefined roles and centralized coordination, limiting their adaptability to evolving challenges. This paper introduces MorphAgent, a novel Autonomous, Self-Organizing, and Self-Adaptive Multi-Agent System for decentralized agent collaboration that enables agents to dynamically evolve their roles and capabilities. Our approach employs self-evolving agent profiles, optimized through three key metrics, guiding agents in refining their individual expertise while maintaining complementary team dynamics. MorphAgent implements a two-phase process: a Profile Update phase for profile optimization, followed by a Task Execution phase where agents continuously adapt their roles based on task feedback. Our experimental results show that MorphAgent outperforms existing frameworks in terms of task performance and adaptability to changing requirements, paving the way for more robust and versatile multi-agent collaborative systems.

keyInsights: Integrating quantitative metrics (RCS, RDS, TRAS) is crucial for guiding agent profile evolution, fostering emergent specialization, and achieving effective self-organization and task alignment.

Decentralized systems with self-evolving agent profiles can achieve significantly higher robustness and resilience in the face of agent failures and dynamic task requirements compared to centrally controlled or rigidly structured MAS.

Embodying principles of individual autonomy, self-organization, and self-adaptability, inspired by natural swarms, allows LLM-based agents to dynamically adapt and optimize their collective behavior without explicit programming or central oversight.
originalProblem: Existing LLM-based Multi-Agent Systems (MAS) typically rely on predefined roles, centralized coordination, or rigid organizational structures, limiting their adaptability and robustness in dynamic environments.
Current MAS struggle to adapt to evolving tasks, unforeseen challenges, or agent failures, due to a lack of individual autonomy, self-organization, and self-adaptability.
A lack of quantitative metrics makes it difficult to systematically evaluate and optimize agent roles and collaboration patterns in decentralized MAS.
results: MORPHAGENT consistently outperformed state-of-the-art baselines (GPTSwarm, AgentVerse, AFLOW) across various benchmark tasks, including Code Generation, General Reasoning, and Mathematical Reasoning.

The system demonstrated significantly higher robustness to agent failures, maintaining 40.10% to 54.00% accuracy across tasks even at an 80% agent failure probability, whereas baselines dropped to 1.43% to 19.52%.

Ablation studies confirmed that the combined use of all three profile optimization metrics (RCS, RDS, TRAS) is essential for optimal performance, achieving 52.00% accuracy with GPT-4o-mini compared to 50.67% without any optimization.
solution: A novel decentralized collaboration framework where LLM-based agents utilize a Dynamic Profile Mechanism, allowing them to continuously refine their roles, capabilities, and interaction rules based on local observations and peer interactions.

Integration of three core principles from natural swarm intelligence: Individual Autonomy (agents make independent decisions), Self-Organization (dynamic formation of collaboration patterns), and Self-Adaptability (agents adjust roles and behaviors over time).

A two-phase iterative process (Profile Update and Task Execution) guided by three quantitative optimization metrics: Role Clarity Score (RCS), Role Differentiation Score (RDS), and Task-Role Alignment Score (TRAS), which provide adaptive feedback for profile evolution.
paper_summary.summary: MORPHAGENT is a decentralized multi-agent system that enables large language model (LLM) agents to autonomously evolve their profiles and adapt their roles in real-time, achieving superior performance and robustness in dynamic and failure-prone environments compared to existing methods. The system maintains high accuracy even with 80% agent failure rates and adapts effectively to domain shifts, leveraging quantitative metrics to guide profile optimization.

title: MorphAgent: Empowering Agents through Self-Evolving Profiles and Decentralized Collaboration

abstract:
Large Language Models (LLMs) have revolutionized Natural Language Processing
but exhibit limitations, particularly in autonomously addressing novel
challenges such as reasoning and problem-solving. Traditional techniques like
chain-of-thought prompting necessitate explicit human guidance. This paper
introduces a novel multi-agent communication framework, inspired by the CAMEL
model, to enhance LLMs' autonomous problem-solving capabilities. The framework
employs multiple LLM agents, each with a distinct persona, engaged in
role-playing communication, offering a nuanced and adaptable approach to
diverse problem scenarios. Extensive experimentation demonstrates the
framework's superior performance and adaptability, providing valuable insights
into the collaborative potential of multiple agents in overcoming the
limitations of individual models.

keyInsights: Collaborative multi-agent systems can mitigate limitations of individual LLMs, such as hallucination and reasoning errors, through iterative refinement.
Assigning distinct personas (e.g., Student, Teacher, Engineer) to LLM agents provides diverse perspectives and structured approaches to problem-solving.
Combining chain-of-thought prompting with inter-agent communication allows for problem decomposition, systematic addressing of sub-problems, and validation of reasoning steps.
originalProblem: Individual Large Language Models (LLMs) frequently generate incorrect information (hallucination) and struggle with multi-step reasoning tasks.
LLMs have fixed context windows, limiting the amount of information they can process simultaneously for complex problems.
Single LLMs typically lack robust mechanisms for self-correction and verifying their own outputs.
results: The multi-agent framework achieved 65% accuracy on the GSM8K dataset, an improvement over the 50% for a single-agent model.
On the SVAMP dataset, the framework demonstrated 77% accuracy, surpassing the 70% of the single-agent model.
For commonsense reasoning, the system reached 83% accuracy on the CSQA dataset, compared to 77% for the single-agent baseline.
solution: A multi-agent communication framework is proposed where multiple LLM agents collaborate to solve problems.
Each agent is assigned a distinct persona (e.g., Student, Teacher) to guide their approach and interaction.
Agents utilize chain-of-thought prompting and engage in structured role-playing communication to share understanding, propose solutions, and critique reasoning.
paper_summary.summary: The LLM Harmony framework, developed by Sumedh Rasal at Georgia Institute of Technology, enhances Large Language Model problem-solving by employing a collaborative multi-agent system. This framework assigns distinct personas to LLM agents and facilitates inter-agent communication alongside chain-of-thought prompting. The approach improved accuracy on arithmetic reasoning (e.g., 65% on GSM8K vs. 50% for single-agent) and commonsense reasoning tasks (83% on CSQA vs. 77% for single-agent).

title: LLM Harmony: Multi-Agent Communication for Problem Solving

abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used to evolve programs and multi-agent systems, yet most existing approaches rely on overwrite-based mutations that maintain only a single candidate at a time. Such methods discard useful variants, suffer from destructive edits, and explore a brittle search space prone to structural failure. We introduce EvoLattice, a framework that represents an entire population of candidate programs or agent behaviors within a single directed acyclic graph. Each node stores multiple persistent alternatives, and every valid path through the graph defines a distinct executable candidate, yielding a large combinatorial search space without duplicating structure. EvoLattice enables fine-grained alternative-level evaluation by scoring each alternative across all paths in which it appears, producing statistics that reveal how local design choices affect global performance. These statistics provide a dense, data-driven feedback signal for LLM-guided mutation, recombination, and pruning, while preserving successful components. Structural correctness is guaranteed by a deterministic self-repair mechanism that enforces acyclicity and dependency consistency independently of the LLM. EvoLattice naturally extends to agent evolution by interpreting alternatives as prompt fragments or sub-agent behaviors. Across program synthesis (proxy and optimizer meta-learning), EvoLattice yields more stable evolution, greater expressivity, and stronger improvement trajectories than prior LLM-guided methods. The resulting dynamics resemble quality-diversity optimization, emerging implicitly from EvoLattice's internal multi-alternative representation rather than an explicit external archive.
title: EvoLattice: Persistent Internal-Population Evolution through Multi-Alternative Quality-Diversity Graph Representations for LLM-Guided Program Discovery

abstract: The advent of Large Language Models (LLMs) has opened new frontiers in automated algorithm design, giving rise to numerous powerful methods. However, these approaches retain critical limitations: they require extensive evaluation of the target problem to guide the search process, making them impractical for real-world optimization tasks, where each evaluation consumes substantial computational resources. This research proposes an innovative and efficient framework that decouples algorithm discovery from high-cost evaluation. Our core innovation lies in combining a Genetic Programming (GP) function generator with an LLM-driven evolutionary algorithm designer. The evolutionary direction of the GP-based function generator is guided by the similarity between the landscape characteristics of generated proxy functions and those of real-world problems, ensuring that algorithms discovered via proxy functions exhibit comparable performance on real-world problems. Our method enables deep exploration of the algorithmic space before final validation while avoiding costly real-world evaluations. We validated the framework's efficacy across multiple real-world problems, demonstrating its ability to discover high-performance algorithms while substantially reducing expensive evaluations. This approach shows a path to apply LLM-based automated algorithm design to computationally intensive real-world optimization challenges.

keyInsights: The use of Genetic Programming guided by Exploratory Landscape Analysis (ELA) features and Wasserstein distance successfully generates proxy functions that accurately capture the essential landscape characteristics of real-world problems.
Decoupling the intensive algorithm discovery phase, typically requiring many evaluations, from direct real-world problem interaction, dramatically reduces the computational budget needed.

Proxy functions can serve as effective "information filters," potentially leading to the discovery of more robust and generalized algorithms by abstracting noise present in direct real-world evaluations.
originalProblem: Current automated algorithm design (AAD) methods, especially those leveraging Large Language Models (LLMs), face prohibitive computational costs due to the need for extensive real-world problem evaluations.
Real-world optimization tasks (e.g., engineering simulations) often have evaluation costs ranging from minutes to days, making direct LLM-based algorithm discovery impractical.
A critical bottleneck exists in making LLM-based AAD a viable solution for high-dimensional, multimodal, and noisy real-world problems under strict evaluation budget constraints.
results: GP-generated proxy functions demonstrated superior landscape similarity, achieving significantly lower average Wasserstein distances to real-world problem ELA features compared to BBOB benchmark functions (e.g., 4.46 vs. 50.81 for meta-surface).

Algorithms discovered through the proxy-driven LLaMEA framework consistently achieved superior Area Over Convergence Curve (AOCC) values on five challenging real-world problems from photonics and optical engineering.

The proxy-driven approach often outperformed established meta-heuristics (DE, LSHADE) and even direct real-world optimization in some cases, delivering physically sound and high-quality solutions within a stringent 50 x D evaluation budget.
solution: A two-stage framework that first characterizes a real-world problem's fitness landscape using Exploratory Landscape Analysis (ELA) features.
Genetic Programming (GP) then evolves low-cost mathematical expressions (proxy functions) that closely match these ELA features, using Wasserstein distance as a fitness metric.

An LLM-driven AAD framework (LLaMEA) subsequently discovers and refines optimization algorithms by evaluating them on these GP-generated proxy functions, minimizing direct interaction with the expensive real-world problem.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Leiden University and A*STAR developed an efficient framework that decouples Large Language Model (LLM)-driven algorithm design from expensive real-world evaluations by generating landscape-aware proxy functions with Genetic Programming. The approach enabled LLMs to discover high-performance optimization algorithms for computationally intensive problems in photonics and optical engineering, often outperforming direct optimization and established meta-heuristics while dramatically reducing real-world evaluation costs.

title: Landscape-aware Automated Algorithm Design: An Efficient Framework for Real-world Optimization

abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have made significant progress in handling complex programming tasks. However, current methods rely on manual model selection and fixed workflows, which limit their ability to adapt to changing task complexities. To address this, we propose SEMAG, a Self-Evolutionary Multi-Agent code Generation framework that mimics human coding practices. It decomposes programming tasks into stages, including planning, coding, debugging, and discussion, while adapting workflows to task difficulty. Its self-evolutionary agents can access the latest models in real time and automatically upgrade the backbone model. SEMAG sets new state-of-the-art Pass@1 accuracy across benchmarks. Using identical backbone models, SEMAG outperforms prior methods by 3.3% on CodeContests. When augmented with self-evolutionary model selection that automatically identifies optimal backbones, SEMAG reaches 52.6%, showcasing both framework effectiveness and adaptability to evolving LLM capabilities.
title: SEMAG: Self-Evolutionary Multi-Agent Code Generation

abstract: Multi-agent systems powered by large language models have demonstrated remarkable capabilities across diverse domains, yet existing automated design approaches seek monolithic solutions that fail to adapt resource allocation based on query complexity and domain requirements. This paper introduces AutoMaAS, a self-evolving multi-agent architecture search framework that leverages neural architecture search principles to automatically discover optimal agent configurations through dynamic operator lifecycle management and automated machine learning techniques. Our approach incorporates four key innovations: (1) automatic operator generation, fusion, and elimination based on performance-cost analysis, (2) dynamic cost-aware optimization with real-time parameter adjustment, (3) online feedback integration for continuous architecture refinement, and (4) enhanced interpretability through decision tracing mechanisms. Extensive experiments across six benchmarks demonstrate that AutoMaAS achieves 1.0-7.1\% performance improvement while reducing inference costs by 3-5\% compared to state-of-the-art methods. The framework shows superior transferability across datasets and LLM backbones, establishing a new paradigm for automated multi-agent system design in the era of large language models.

keyInsights: Dynamic adaptation of multi-agent LLM architectures, tailored per query, significantly enhances performance and resource efficiency over static designs.
A self-evolving operator pool, capable of generating, fusing, and eliminating operators based on their health and utility, is critical for maintaining system adaptability in dynamic environments.
